1: <header>A life cycle assessment of seasonal grass-based and confinement dairy farms </header>

Donal O'Brien a,b Laurence Shalloo a,* Joe Patton a Frank Buckley Chris Grainger Michael Wallace b a Livestock Systems Research Department, Animal & Grassland Research and Innovation Centre, Teagasc, Moorepark, Fermoy, Co. Cork, Ireland b School of Agriculture, Food Science and Veterinary Medicine, University College Dublin, Belfield, Dublin 4, Ireland

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 19 April 2011 Received in revised form 14 November 2011 Accepted 16 November 2011 Available online 12 January 2012 Keywords: Life cycle assessment Grass-based Confinement Milk Concentrate Environmental impact|Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a holistic systems approach that aims to assess the environmental impacts (potential pollutants and resource use) of the production of goods and services. The aim of this study was to develop an LCA model to compare contrasting milk production systems, a seasonal pasture-based dairy farm and a confinement dairy farm. The environmental impacts considered were global warming, eutro- phication, acidification, land use and non-renewable energy use. The LCA estimated on-farm, off-farm (pollutants and resources associated with the production and supply of purchased farm inputs) and total (on-farm and off-farm) environmental impacts. Environmental impacts were quantified per unit of milk and per unit area. The study only considered two research farms, because high quality data were unavail- able for a large number of farms. Thus, this was not a representative LCA comparison. The genetic merit of cows modelled was similar for each system. A total mixed ration was fed in the confinement system and grazed grass was mainly fed in the grass-based system. Research data were used to confirm simulated dry matter intake (DMI) and predicted enteric CH4 output from simulated DMI. The study found that when expressed per unit of milk and per on-farm area, all total environmental impacts were greater for the con- finement system compared to the grass-based system. Per total farm area (on-farm and off-farm area), all environmental impacts except global warming were lower for the grass-based system. The greater envi- ronmental impact of the confinement dairy system was due to the greater use of concentrate feed and the longer manure storage period. Scenario modelling demonstrated that there is potential to decrease the environmental impact of dairy systems, particularly the confinement system, by reducing the use of con- centrate ingredients with a high environmental impact and by storing manure in solid systems. Scenario modelling also showed that assumptions regarding the carbon cycle should be clearly outlined when assessing milk production systems and that standardisation of LCA allocation procedures is required. This LCA study is one of the few to directly compare the environmental impact of a grass-based and a confine- ment dairy system. However direct comparisons are needed, using an LCA methodology such as described in this paper, and using data from a greater number of farms so that each system is better rep- resented thereby ensuring a robust comparison of the two systems on a regional or national basis. 2011 Elsevier Ltd. 2: The planned removal of the European Union (EU) milk quota sys- tem in 2015 is expected to result in an increase in milk output and decline in milk price (Binfield et al., 2008; Bouamra-Mechemache et al., 2008; Lips and Rieder, 2005). Previous analysis shows that post the abolition of the EU milk quota system, land area will be the new key factor limiting production for grass-based dairy producers (Dillon et al., 2005; O'Donnell et al., 2008). Under these circum- stances, one option for grass-based dairy producers would be to in- crease amounts of supplementary concentrates and forages, which would allow expansion of the dairy enterprise on the available graz- ing platform. In cases where land availability is a major impediment, producers may decide to adopt alternative production strategies

such as confinement systems using a total mixed ration (TMR). Dairy producers will also have to meet increasing environmental regula- tions in the future including limits on greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions and noxious gaseous emissions such as ammonia (NH3), and greater nutrient management regulations to control diffuse pollu- tion from nitrate (NO) leaching and phosphate (PO - - run-off (European Council, 1991; Lovett et al., 2008; Powers, 2003). Thus, considering the increasing emphasis on minimising the environ- mental impact (potential pollutants and resource use) of food pro- duction and the effects of removing milk quota as a constraint, analysis is required to quantify the effect that grass-based and con- finement milk production systems have on the environment. In recent years, life cycle assessment (LCA) has become a popu- lar globally accepted method to assess the impact of agricultural systems on the environment (Thomassen et al., 2008b). Life cycle assessment takes a systems approach to appraising the environ- mental consequences of a product for its entire life cycle. The

predominant environmental consequences quantified in LCA stud- ies of dairy systems are the acidifying and eutrophic effects of milk production on watercourses, the global warming effect of dairy systems and the utilisation of resources such as land and non-renewable energy during the production of milk. Typically, LCA studies of agricultural systems do not assess the complete life cycle of a product. Instead the effect of the agricultural system on the environment is evaluated up to the point the primary product is sold from the farm (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Williams et al., 2006). Previous, LCA studies of dairy farming systems have estimated environmental impacts from conventional seasonal grass-based systems (Basset-Mens et al., 2009b; Haas et al., 2001; Van der Werf et al., 2009) but few studies have used the approach to estimate the environmental impact of confinement feeding operations (Arsenault et al., 2009; Rotz et al., 2009).

The objective of this study was to develop an LCA model to quantify and compare the environmental impact of a confinement dairy farm and an intensively grazed seasonal grass-based dairy system. A secondary objective of this study was to identify the main contributors to environmental impacts in each system.

6: The input data required (Table 1) for completing an LCA of a seasonal grass-based dairy system and a confinement dairy system was based on the research of Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009) conducted at Moorepark dairy production research centre, Fermoy, Co Cork, Ireland (52°16'N, 8°25'W) from December 2006 to January 2008. The objective of these studies was to investigate the effects that a seasonal grass-based and confinement dairy system have on the reproductive performance and milk yield of Holstein-Friesian cows.
Technical description of the seasonal grass-based dairy system (Grass) and the confinement dairy system (confinement).

Item|Unit|Grass|Confinement Farm size|ha|40|20 Milking cows|#|90|90 Total FPCMb production|kg FPCM/cow per year|6639|8040 Delivered FPCM|kg FPCM/cow per year|6538|7942 Milk fat|%|4.31|4.15 Milk protein|%|3.49|3.37 Length of lactation|days|305|305 Replacement rate|%|18|18 Cull rate|%|18|18 Average bodyweight|kg|567|609 Stocking rate|luc/ha|2.54|5.10 Concentrate|kg DM/cow per year|370|2865 Grass|kg DM/cow per year|4093|0 Grass silage|kg DM/cow per year|1063|1497 Maize silage|kg DM/cow per year|0|1746 Barley straw|kg DM/cow per year|0|499 Concentrate|t DM/herd per year|42|249 Grass|t DM/herd per year|381|34 Grass silage|t DM/herd per year|103|143 Maize silage|t DM/herd per year|0|147 Barley straw|t DM/herd per year|0|43 N fertiliser|kg N/ha per year|260|85 Annual manure exported|%|0|23

a Data based on research of Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009). b FPCM = fat and protein corrected milk = (0.337 + 0.116 X %fat + 0.06 X %pro- tein) X kg milk (Thomassen and de Boer, 2005). C lu = Livestock unit equivalent to 1 dairy cow. d Forage intakes were estimated with the Moorepark Dairy System Model (Shalloo et al., 2004) using milk production, animal bodyweight, concentrate sup- plementation and feed ration composition data from Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al., (2009).

In both studies, spring-calving Holstein-Friesian cows were blocked based on genetic merit (Irish Economic Breeding Index €60 [+17.3]), parity (2.5 [+1.49]), expected calving date (6th March [+27.9]), body condition score (3.0 [+0.69]) and predicted milk yield (+98 [+123.4]) and assigned randomly from within pairs to either the grass-based or confinement system. The aim of the grass-based system was to maximize the use of grazed grass in the feed budget of lactating dairy cows. This was achieved by tar- geting calving to commence in early spring (mean calving date 14th of February) which is generally the onset of the grass-growing season (Dillon et al., 1995). Grass silage was harvested on-farm when grass growth exceeded herd feed demand. The system was self-sufficient for forage. The fertiliser input (N) was 260 kg/ha. Grass silage and concentrate were offered during periods when pasture growth was unable to meet the nutritional requirements of the herd. The annual concentrate offered was 370 kg of dry mat- ter (DM) per cow. Concentrate was mainly offered in early and late lactation. Grass silage was predominately fed during the dry period along with minerals and vitamins.

Within the confinement system, cows were housed full time and fed a TMR. However, replacement animals were only indoors during the winter period and grazed pasture during the growing season. Grass silage was harvested on-farm and any other feed used was imported. Holstein-Friesian cows were offered specific TMR diets during the dry period and lactation. The TMR offered during the dry period consisted of (g/kg DM) maize silage (247), grass silage (247), barley straw (416), soybean meal (80) and min- eral and vitamins (10). During lactation, the TMR offered consisted of maize silage (267), grass silage (223), molasses (55), barley straw (20) and concentrate (436). The concentrate portion of the TMR consisted of (g/kg DM) soybean meal (325), rolled barley (290), citrus pulp (190), maize gluten (160), vegetable oil (25) and mineral supplement (10). The TMR offered was comparable in nutrient composition to TMR fed in other studies (Grainger et al., 2009; Kolver and Muller, 1998), apart from DM content, which was lower than other studies (Bargo et al., 2002; Vibart et al., 2008). The low DM content was mainly due to the low DM of the maize and grass silages offered. The maize silage used was typical of maize produced in Ireland during a poor growing season with low DM content, poorly developed ears, and low starch con- tent (Fitzgerald and Murphy, 1999).

Milk yield data, animal weight, concentrate supplementation data and the composition of the feed ration were used with the Moorepark Dairy System Model (MDSM; Shalloo et al., 2004) to simulate the total dry matter intake (DMI) of Holstein-Friesian cows. Simulated feed intakes were verified with measured data from Patton et al. (2009). Olmos et al. (2009) reported that the over- all pregnancy rate was not significantly different between systems; therefore the replacement rate did not differ between systems (Table 1). In both systems, bull calves were sold soon after they were born.

(2009) conducted at Moorepark dairy production research centre, Fermoy, Co Cork, Ireland (52°16'N, 8°25'W) from December 2006 to January 2008. The objective of these studies was to investigate the effects that a seasonal grass-based and confinement dairy system have on the reproductive performance and milk yield of Holstein-Friesian cows.

a Data based on research of Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009). b FPCM = fat and protein corrected milk = (0.337 + 0.116 X %fat + 0.06 X %pro- tein) X kg milk (Thomassen and de Boer, 2005). C lu = Livestock unit equivalent to 1 dairy cow. d Forage intakes were estimated with the Moorepark Dairy System Model (Shalloo et al., 2004) using milk production, animal bodyweight, concentrate sup- plementation and feed ration composition data from Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al., (2009).

In both studies, spring-calving Holstein-Friesian cows were blocked based on genetic merit (Irish Economic Breeding Index €60 [+17.3]), parity (2.5 [+1.49]), expected calving date (6th March [+27.9]), body condition score (3.0 [+0.69]) and predicted milk yield (+98 [+123.4]) and assigned randomly from within pairs to either the grass-based or confinement system. The aim of the grass-based system was to maximize the use of grazed grass in the feed budget of lactating dairy cows. This was achieved by tar- geting calving to commence in early spring (mean calving date 14th of February) which is generally the onset of the grass-growing season (Dillon et al., 1995). Grass silage was harvested on-farm when grass growth exceeded herd feed demand. The system was self-sufficient for forage. The fertiliser input (N) was 260 kg/ha. Grass silage and concentrate were offered during periods when pasture growth was unable to meet the nutritional requirements of the herd. The annual concentrate offered was 370 kg of dry mat- ter (DM) per cow. Concentrate was mainly offered in early and late lactation. Grass silage was predominately fed during the dry period along with minerals and vitamins.

Within the confinement system, cows were housed full time and fed a TMR. However, replacement animals were only indoors during the winter period and grazed pasture during the growing season. Grass silage was harvested on-farm and any other feed used was imported. Holstein-Friesian cows were offered specific TMR diets during the dry period and lactation. The TMR offered during the dry period consisted of (g/kg DM) maize silage (247), grass silage (247), barley straw (416), soybean meal (80) and min- eral and vitamins (10). During lactation, the TMR offered consisted of maize silage (267), grass silage (223), molasses (55), barley straw (20) and concentrate (436). The concentrate portion of the TMR consisted of (g/kg DM) soybean meal (325), rolled barley (290), citrus pulp (190), maize gluten (160), vegetable oil (25) and mineral supplement (10). The TMR offered was comparable in nutrient composition to TMR fed in other studies (Grainger et al., 2009; Kolver and Muller, 1998), apart from DM content, which was lower than other studies (Bargo et al., 2002; Vibart et al., 2008). The low DM content was mainly due to the low DM of the maize and grass silages offered. The maize silage used was typical of maize produced in Ireland during a poor growing season with low DM content, poorly developed ears, and low starch con- tent (Fitzgerald and Murphy, 1999).

Milk yield data, animal weight, concentrate supplementation data and the composition of the feed ration were used with the Moorepark Dairy System Model (MDSM; Shalloo et al., 2004) to simulate the total dry matter intake (DMI) of Holstein-Friesian cows. Simulated feed intakes were verified with measured data from Patton et al. (2009). Olmos et al. (2009) reported that the over- all pregnancy rate was not significantly different between systems; therefore the replacement rate did not differ between systems (Table 1). In both systems, bull calves were sold soon after they were born.

(2009) conducted at Moorepark dairy production research centre, Fermoy, Co Cork, Ireland (52°16'N, 8°25'W) from December 2006 to January 2008. The objective of these studies was to investigate the effects that a seasonal grass-based and confinement dairy system have on the reproductive performance and milk yield of Holstein-Friesian cows.

12: Life cycle assessment is a compilation and appraisal of the in- puts, outputs and possible environmental impacts of a production system throughout its life cycle (Guinee et al., 2002). The subse- quent description of the methodology is arranged into the four phases of LCA according to the ISO standards 14040 and 14044 (ISO, 2006a,b): (i) goal and scope, (ii) life cycle inventory, (iii) life cycle impact assessment and (iv) life cycle interpretation.
13: The initial stage, goal and scope definition, entails stating the objective of the study and defining the system boundaries, func- tional unit and method of allocation (ISO, 2006a). The goal of this

study was to develop an LCA model using the LCA software Simapro (Pré Consultants, 2008), to assess the environmental impact of an intensively managed seasonal grass-based dairy farm compared to a confinement dairy farm. Given the limited data set used for gener- ating the grass-based and confinement dairy farms, it was not in- tended to develop a robust comparison of the two systems. The system boundaries of the LCA model developed were set at the point milk was sold from the farm i.e. "cradle to farm gate". Therefore, the environmental impacts of all processes involved up to the point milk is sold from the farm, such as the production and transport of fertil- iser and concentrates, were included. Production of some inputs, for instance medicine, were excluded because of their small effect on the environmental impact of milk production. The temporal cover- age of the LCA model developed was a period of 1 year and the mode of LCA analysis used was attributional (descriptive).

Dairy farms are multifunctional systems producing food, pro- viding environmental services (e.g. maintaining and enhancing wildlife habitats) and generating income for the farmer. According to these functions, four functional units were selected: tonne of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) sold, tonne of milk solids (MS) sold, on-farm area occupied and total farm area occupied (farm- land and land required for purchased fodder and concentrate). The ISO (2006a) standards were followed when allocating environ- mental impacts of multifunctional processes. Multifunctional pro- cesses were the production of concentrate co-products such as palm oil and palm kernel meal and production of milk and meat on-farm. The concentrate co-products used by farming system, their share in concentrate blends and their origin of production is shown in Table 2. Economic allocation factors based on their rel- ative economic value were used to allocate environmental impacts between concentrate co-products (Table 2). Alternative allocation rules based on the mass of concentrate co-products were also con- sidered to test the sensitivity of results to alternative allocation rules (Table 2).

Similar to previous studies, environmental impacts were allo- cated between the co-products milk and meat based on the strong causal relationship between the feed ration of the dairy cow and her two outputs milk and meat (Basset-Mens et al., 2009b; Ceder- berg and Mattsson, 2000; O'Brien et al., 2010). The allocation factor was estimated based on the energy and protein requirements of the herd, which was calculated by the MDSM (Shalloo et al., 2004). The proportion of the total energy and protein requirements of the herd used to produce meat in the grass-based and confine-

19: <header>2.2.2. Life cycle inventory </header>

In the inventory analysis stage, materials used and pollutants related to each production process were quantified. The LCA model built within Simapro (Pré Consultants, 2008) was used during this stage to quantify on-farm and off-farm resource use and pollutants. Data collected on farm by Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009) were predominately used to determine the quantity of materials used directly on farm, for instance the amount of fertiliser used. The work of Rice and Quinlan (2003) was used to estimate farm energy use for grassland management, fertiliser spreading, grass silage production and slurry spreading. The report of Nemecek and Kägi (2007) was used to estimate energy required for the production of silage other than grass. The review of Dalgaard et al. (2001) was used to estimate energy consumption for the loading and handling, together with the subsequent distri- bution of feedstuffs.

On-farm, carbon is assimilated or emitted as carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4). Potential sinks of carbon are soil carbon sequestration and pastoral photosynthesis. However, the model as- sumes that no assimilation of carbon occurs. Carbon sequestration was excluded because most pastures within Ireland are semi or permanent pasture. Therefore, based on the IPCC (2006), the car- bon flux from the soil is most likely to be in equilibrium. Pastoral photosynthesis was not considered because these simulations are based on productive grasslands where carbon fixing through pho- tosynthesis would approximate that of carbon oxidation through senescence and decay. Emissions of CO2 were limited to fuel com- bustion, lime and urea application as the carbon balance of soil was assumed to be in equilibrium. Diesel is the main fuel used on-farm. Carbon dioxide from this source was estimated as 2.62 kg CO2/1 diesel (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Diesel combustion also emits other gases including sulphur dioxide (SO2), NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx) and nitrous oxide (N20) and uses finite energy. Emissions of these gases were estimated using data from Nemecek and Kägi (2007) and finite energy use was estimated as 36.4 MJ/1 of diesel (Howley et al., 2009). Carbon dioxide from lime and urea use was estimated as 0.12 kg CO2-C/kg of lime and 0.2 kg CO2-C/kg of urea, respectively (IPCC, 2006).

Methane emission arises from feed digestion (enteric fermenta- tion) and manure management. Fixed emission factors that esti- mate that 6.5% of gross energy intake is lost as CH4 (MJ/day) were used to estimate enteric CH4 from cows when grazed grass was the major dietary component for the dairy cow (>50% of DMI; O'Mara, 2006) and for all dietary intakes for 1-2-year old heifers and calves (IPCC, 2006). When grass was not the major die- tary component of the cow, the following Mitscherlich algorithm from Mills et al. (2003) was used:
CH4 (MJ/day) = CH4 max min) exp kn(-0.0011x(ADF/Starch)+0.0045)xMEI (1)
where CH4 max is the maximum possible enteric CH4 emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), CH4 min is the minimum possible enteric CH4

emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), MEI is the metabolisable energy intake (MJ/day) and ADF is acid detergent fibre. This method of computing CH4 from enteric fermentation requires data on animal weight, body weight change, animal activity, feed digestibility, milk production and composition, and replacement rate of cows. The MDSM was linked to the LCA model to obtain this information. Combining the MDSM with the LCA model provided data on animal and feed variables and was used to compute feed intakes according to the net energy required for milk production, maintenance and body weight change (Jarrige, 1989). These feed variables were also used with the digestibility of feed to estimate animal manure pro- duction. The quantity of manure requiring storage was calculated based on the number of days animals spent housed.

Methane emission from manure management arises during storage and following application on land. Methane is also released from manure deposited by animals on pasture but the quantities are negligible (IPCC, 2006), and as a result this source was ex- cluded. Methane from stored liquid manure and solid manure (>15% DM) was estimated as 22 g CH4-C/m³ per day and 5.9 g CH4-C/m³ per day, respectively (Husted, 1994; Sneath et al., 2006). Methane from stored silage effluent was estimated as 0.165 kg CH4/106 biological oxygen demand (US EPA, 1990). The season dependent emission factor of Chadwick et al. (2000) was used for quantifying methane emission from manure spreading. Methane from slurry spreading in spring, summer and autumn was estimated using the emission factors 12 g CH4/m3 0.07 g CH4/m3 and 6.8 g CH4/m3, respectively.

Nitrogen emissions from animals were derived following the quantification of N excreted. Nitrogen excreted from animals was estimated as the difference between the total N intake (calculated as the dietary DM intake and the N content of the diet) and the N output in product (meat, milk). The equations of Kebreab et al. (2001) were used to separate N excretion between faeces and ur- line. The quantity of N stored was estimated based on the time the animal spent indoors. Ammonia, NO, N20 and NO were the main nitrogen emissions quantified.
On-farm, NH3 volatilises during grazing, housing, manure stor- age and during the application of organic and inorganic fertilisers.

The mass flow model of Webb and Misselbrook (2004) was adopted to quantify NH3 emission related to manure. This ap- proach estimates NH3 from a pool of total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) in livestock excreta where the TAN pool is estimated from the annual N excreted by ruminants, the proportion of annual N excreted in the form of TAN (60%; Webb, 2001) and the number of livestock. The TAN pool is then partitioned between manure deposited in housing and on pasture based on the length of the grazing season. Total ammoniacal nitrogen deposited in animal housing is tracked through the different manure management stages (housing, storage, spreading). Estimates are made for each stage, with emission factors from Table 3. Emission expressed as g NH3-N/UK livestock unit (luuk) per day (a UK livestock unit is de- fined as 500 kg bodyweight) were converted to a proportion of TAN excreted using the following equation from Webb and Misselbrook (2004): EF%TAN = (EFluuk/1000) X (Y/500) (100/TAN) (2)

where EF%TAN is the emission factor expressed as a percentage of the TAN pool, is the emission factor in g NH3-N/luuk per day, Y is the mean bodyweight per animal (kg), d is number of housing days and TAN is the annual TAN excretion per animal (kg). Ammonia emission quantified at each stage was subtracted from the TAN pool to determine TAN available for the next stage, e.g. NH3 emission from livestock housing reduce the size of the TAN pool in manure storage.

Volatilisation of NH3 from the application of inorganic N fertil- isers were calculated using fixed emission factors and sub-divided into those applied to grassland and arable land (Hyde et al., 2003). The emission factors used for applications to grassland were 23%, 1.6%, and 1.6% of the applied N for urea, calcium ammonium ni- trate (CAN) and other inorganic N applications, respectively, and were assumed to be 50% less for applications to arable land.

Nitrous oxide is produced as an intermediate product in the denitrification process or as a by-product in the nitrification pro- cess. During the denitrification process NO may also be emitted. Nitrous oxide emissions generated on-farm occurs directly from manure storage and application, manure deposited by grazing

cattle on pasture, spreading of inorganic fertilisers, indirectly fol- lowing nitrate leaching and run-off, and following re-deposition of volatilised gases to soils and waters (IPCC, 2006). Emissions of N20 from stored liquid and solid manure were estimated using the emission factors 0.01 g N2O-N/m³ per day and 0.4 N2O-N/ m³ per day, respectively (Sneath et al., 2006).

After subtraction of gaseous N losses during housing, manure storage, and manure spreading, N20 emission from the application of manure to land was estimated. Nitrous oxide emission for slurry spreading in autumn or winter was estimated as 1.10% of available N applied (Chadwick et al., 2011). However, the N20 emission fac- tor for spring or summer application was 0.51% of available N ap- plied; because crops are growing during this period creating a bigger N sink (Chadwick et al., 2011). An emission factor of 0.2% was used to estimate N20 emission from the application of solid manure (Chadwick et al., 2011). The global default estimate factor for N20 from manure deposited by grazing animals is 2% of N ex- creted (IPCC, 2006). However, research conducted in temperate re- gions estimates that 1% of N deposited during grazing is typically emitted as N20 (Galbally et al., 2010; Van Groenigen et al., 2005). Thus, based on the location of farms in this study it was estimated that 1% of N deposited during grazing is emitted as N20. Emission of N20 from the application of artificial fertiliser was estimated as 1% of N applied (IPCC, 2006).

Indirect N20 following re-deposition of NH3 to soil and water was calculated as 1% of the total NH3 volatilised on-farm (IPCC, 2006). Nitrous oxide emission from nitrate leached was calculated as 0.75% of total N leached on-farm (IPCC, 2006). On-farm emis- sions of NO were estimated as 21% of N20 emissions (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). The equation includes direct NO from fertilisers and the soil. Potential leaching of N and P to water was computed by means of a farm gate balance approach. According to this ap- proach, N and P available for leaching and surface run-off was quantified as total inputs minus the N or P removed in product minus the N lost directly to the atmosphere. This indicator repre- sents the N or P surplus (Oenema et al., 2005). The fraction of N lost to water was estimated as 10% of the N surplus, which was based on research studies and a national monitoring program measuring N loss to waterways (del Prado et al., 2006; Neut, 1999). The P sur- plus lost to waterways was estimated as 0.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was between 1 and 10 kg, 1.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus was between 10 and 20 kg, and 2.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was > 20 kg (Schulte et al., 2010).

The data collected and estimated for on-farm resource use were used to quantify environmental impacts associated with purchased inputs. The emissions associated with the manufacture of these external inputs were linearly related to the amount used on-farm, e.g. off-farm CO2 emission from fertiliser production is dependant

on the amount and type of fertiliser used on-farm (Van der Werf et al., 2009). Data on the environmental impact associated with the production and supply of these inputs were based on the Eco- invent database (Ecoinvent, 2010) and data from the literature, e.g. Howley et al. (2009). The key inputs purchased on-farm along with the resources used and pollutants released during their manufac- ture are presented in Table 4.

Greenhouse gas emission associated with concentrate produc- tion in Table 4 also includes direct emissions from the conversion of rainforest to agricultural land. Soybean from Brazil and palm fruit bunches from Malaysia were the main concentrate compo- nents responsible for GHG emission from land use change (Ecoin- vent, 2010). The average land use change emission from soybean and palm fruit were estimated by firstly quantifying the total land use change emission caused by soybean and palm cultivation (Jungbluth et al., 2007). The average GHG emissions for soybean and palm fruit from land use change were estimated by dividing the total GHG emission from land use change by the total crop area. This resulted in estimates of 2.6 and 5.5 tonnes of CO2/ha for land use change GHG emission from soybean and palm fruit production, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2010).

In the inventory analysis stage, materials used and pollutants related to each production process were quantified. The LCA model built within Simapro (Pré Consultants, 2008) was used during this stage to quantify on-farm and off-farm resource use and pollutants. Data collected on farm by Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009) were predominately used to determine the quantity of materials used directly on farm, for instance the amount of fertiliser used. The work of Rice and Quinlan (2003) was used to estimate farm energy use for grassland management, fertiliser spreading, grass silage production and slurry spreading. The report of Nemecek and Kägi (2007) was used to estimate energy required for the production of silage other than grass. The review of Dalgaard et al. (2001) was used to estimate energy consumption for the loading and handling, together with the subsequent distri- bution of feedstuffs.

Ammonia, NO, N20 and NO were the main nitrogen emissions quantified.
On-farm, NH3 volatilises during grazing, housing, manure stor- age and during the application of organic and inorganic fertilisers.

The mass flow model of Webb and Misselbrook (2004) was adopted to quantify NH3 emission related to manure. This ap- proach estimates NH3 from a pool of total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) in livestock excreta where the TAN pool is estimated from the annual N excreted by ruminants, the proportion of annual N excreted in the form of TAN (60%; Webb, 2001) and the number of livestock. The TAN pool is then partitioned between manure deposited in housing and on pasture based on the length of the grazing season. Total ammoniacal nitrogen deposited in animal housing is tracked through the different manure management stages (housing, storage, spreading). Estimates are made for each stage, with emission factors from Table 3. Emission expressed as g NH3-N/UK livestock unit (luuk) per day (a UK livestock unit is de- fined as 500 kg bodyweight) were converted to a proportion of TAN excreted using the following equation from Webb and Misselbrook (2004): EF%TAN = (EFluuk/1000) X (Y/500) (100/TAN) (2)

where EF%TAN is the emission factor expressed as a percentage of the TAN pool, is the emission factor in g NH3-N/luuk per day, Y is the mean bodyweight per animal (kg), d is number of housing days and TAN is the annual TAN excretion per animal (kg). Ammonia emission quantified at each stage was subtracted from the TAN pool to determine TAN available for the next stage, e.g. NH3 emission from livestock housing reduce the size of the TAN pool in manure storage.

Volatilisation of NH3 from the application of inorganic N fertil- isers were calculated using fixed emission factors and sub-divided into those applied to grassland and arable land (Hyde et al., 2003). The emission factors used for applications to grassland were 23%, 1.6%, and 1.6% of the applied N for urea, calcium ammonium ni- trate (CAN) and other inorganic N applications, respectively, and were assumed to be 50% less for applications to arable land.

Nitrous oxide is produced as an intermediate product in the denitrification process or as a by-product in the nitrification pro- cess. During the denitrification process NO may also be emitted. Nitrous oxide emissions generated on-farm occurs directly from manure storage and application, manure deposited by grazing

cattle on pasture, spreading of inorganic fertilisers, indirectly fol- lowing nitrate leaching and run-off, and following re-deposition of volatilised gases to soils and waters (IPCC, 2006). Emissions of N20 from stored liquid and solid manure were estimated using the emission factors 0.01 g N2O-N/m³ per day and 0.4 N2O-N/ m³ per day, respectively (Sneath et al., 2006).

After subtraction of gaseous N losses during housing, manure storage, and manure spreading, N20 emission from the application of manure to land was estimated. Nitrous oxide emission for slurry spreading in autumn or winter was estimated as 1.10% of available N applied (Chadwick et al., 2011). However, the N20 emission fac- tor for spring or summer application was 0.51% of available N ap- plied; because crops are growing during this period creating a bigger N sink (Chadwick et al., 2011). An emission factor of 0.2% was used to estimate N20 emission from the application of solid manure (Chadwick et al., 2011). The global default estimate factor for N20 from manure deposited by grazing animals is 2% of N ex- creted (IPCC, 2006). However, research conducted in temperate re- gions estimates that 1% of N deposited during grazing is typically emitted as N20 (Galbally et al., 2010; Van Groenigen et al., 2005). Thus, based on the location of farms in this study it was estimated that 1% of N deposited during grazing is emitted as N20. Emission of N20 from the application of artificial fertiliser was estimated as 1% of N applied (IPCC, 2006).

Indirect N20 following re-deposition of NH3 to soil and water was calculated as 1% of the total NH3 volatilised on-farm (IPCC, 2006). Nitrous oxide emission from nitrate leached was calculated as 0.75% of total N leached on-farm (IPCC, 2006). On-farm emis- sions of NO were estimated as 21% of N20 emissions (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). The equation includes direct NO from fertilisers and the soil. Potential leaching of N and P to water was computed by means of a farm gate balance approach. According to this ap- proach, N and P available for leaching and surface run-off was quantified as total inputs minus the N or P removed in product minus the N lost directly to the atmosphere. This indicator repre- sents the N or P surplus (Oenema et al., 2005). The fraction of N lost to water was estimated as 10% of the N surplus, which was based on research studies and a national monitoring program measuring N loss to waterways (del Prado et al., 2006; Neut, 1999). The P sur- plus lost to waterways was estimated as 0.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was between 1 and 10 kg, 1.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus was between 10 and 20 kg, and 2.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was > 20 kg (Schulte et al., 2010).

The data collected and estimated for on-farm resource use were used to quantify environmental impacts associated with purchased inputs. The emissions associated with the manufacture of these external inputs were linearly related to the amount used on-farm, e.g. off-farm CO2 emission from fertiliser production is dependant

on the amount and type of fertiliser used on-farm (Van der Werf et al., 2009). Data on the environmental impact associated with the production and supply of these inputs were based on the Eco- invent database (Ecoinvent, 2010) and data from the literature, e.g. Howley et al. (2009). The key inputs purchased on-farm along with the resources used and pollutants released during their manufac- ture are presented in Table 4.

Greenhouse gas emission associated with concentrate produc- tion in Table 4 also includes direct emissions from the conversion of rainforest to agricultural land. Soybean from Brazil and palm fruit bunches from Malaysia were the main concentrate compo- nents responsible for GHG emission from land use change (Ecoin- vent, 2010). The average land use change emission from soybean and palm fruit were estimated by firstly quantifying the total land use change emission caused by soybean and palm cultivation (Jungbluth et al., 2007). The average GHG emissions for soybean and palm fruit from land use change were estimated by dividing the total GHG emission from land use change by the total crop area. This resulted in estimates of 2.6 and 5.5 tonnes of CO2/ha for land use change GHG emission from soybean and palm fruit production, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2010).
22: <header>2.2.2. Life cycle inventory </header>

In the inventory analysis stage, materials used and pollutants related to each production process were quantified. The LCA model built within Simapro (Pré Consultants, 2008) was used during this stage to quantify on-farm and off-farm resource use and pollutants. Data collected on farm by Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009) were predominately used to determine the quantity of materials used directly on farm, for instance the amount of fertiliser used. The work of Rice and Quinlan (2003) was used to estimate farm energy use for grassland management, fertiliser spreading, grass silage production and slurry spreading. The report of Nemecek and Kägi (2007) was used to estimate energy required for the production of silage other than grass. The review of Dalgaard et al. (2001) was used to estimate energy consumption for the loading and handling, together with the subsequent distri- bution of feedstuffs.

On-farm, carbon is assimilated or emitted as carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4). Potential sinks of carbon are soil carbon sequestration and pastoral photosynthesis. However, the model as- sumes that no assimilation of carbon occurs. Carbon sequestration was excluded because most pastures within Ireland are semi or permanent pasture. Therefore, based on the IPCC (2006), the car- bon flux from the soil is most likely to be in equilibrium. Pastoral photosynthesis was not considered because these simulations are based on productive grasslands where carbon fixing through pho- tosynthesis would approximate that of carbon oxidation through senescence and decay. Emissions of CO2 were limited to fuel com- bustion, lime and urea application as the carbon balance of soil was assumed to be in equilibrium. Diesel is the main fuel used on-farm. Carbon dioxide from this source was estimated as 2.62 kg CO2/1 diesel (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Diesel combustion also emits other gases including sulphur dioxide (SO2), NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx) and nitrous oxide (N20) and uses finite energy. Emissions of these gases were estimated using data from Nemecek and Kägi (2007) and finite energy use was estimated as 36.4 MJ/1 of diesel (Howley et al., 2009). Carbon dioxide from lime and urea use was estimated as 0.12 kg CO2-C/kg of lime and 0.2 kg CO2-C/kg of urea, respectively (IPCC, 2006).

Methane emission arises from feed digestion (enteric fermenta- tion) and manure management. Fixed emission factors that esti- mate that 6.5% of gross energy intake is lost as CH4 (MJ/day) were used to estimate enteric CH4 from cows when grazed grass was the major dietary component for the dairy cow (>50% of DMI; O'Mara, 2006) and for all dietary intakes for 1-2-year old heifers and calves (IPCC, 2006). When grass was not the major die- tary component of the cow, the following Mitscherlich algorithm from Mills et al. (2003) was used:
CH4 (MJ/day) = CH4 max min) exp kn(-0.0011x(ADF/Starch)+0.0045)xMEI (1)
where CH4 max is the maximum possible enteric CH4 emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), CH4 min is the minimum possible enteric CH4

emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), MEI is the metabolisable energy intake (MJ/day) and ADF is acid detergent fibre. This method of computing CH4 from enteric fermentation requires data on animal weight, body weight change, animal activity, feed digestibility, milk production and composition, and replacement rate of cows. The MDSM was linked to the LCA model to obtain this information. Combining the MDSM with the LCA model provided data on animal and feed variables and was used to compute feed intakes according to the net energy required for milk production, maintenance and body weight change (Jarrige, 1989). These feed variables were also used with the digestibility of feed to estimate animal manure pro- duction. The quantity of manure requiring storage was calculated based on the number of days animals spent housed.

Methane emission from manure management arises during storage and following application on land. Methane is also released from manure deposited by animals on pasture but the quantities are negligible (IPCC, 2006), and as a result this source was ex- cluded. Methane from stored liquid manure and solid manure (>15% DM) was estimated as 22 g CH4-C/m³ per day and 5.9 g CH4-C/m³ per day, respectively (Husted, 1994; Sneath et al., 2006). Methane from stored silage effluent was estimated as 0.165 kg CH4/106 biological oxygen demand (US EPA, 1990). The season dependent emission factor of Chadwick et al. (2000) was used for quantifying methane emission from manure spreading. Methane from slurry spreading in spring, summer and autumn was estimated using the emission factors 12 g CH4/m3 0.07 g CH4/m3 and 6.8 g CH4/m3, respectively.

Nitrogen emissions from animals were derived following the quantification of N excreted. Nitrogen excreted from animals was estimated as the difference between the total N intake (calculated as the dietary DM intake and the N content of the diet) and the N output in product (meat, milk). The equations of Kebreab et al. (2001) were used to separate N excretion between faeces and ur- line. The quantity of N stored was estimated based on the time the animal spent indoors. Ammonia, NO, N20 and NO were the main nitrogen emissions quantified.

where EF%TAN is the emission factor expressed as a percentage of the TAN pool, is the emission factor in g NH3-N/luuk per day, Y is the mean bodyweight per animal (kg), d is number of housing days and TAN is the annual TAN excretion per animal (kg). Ammonia emission quantified at each stage was subtracted from the TAN pool to determine TAN available for the next stage, e.g. NH3 emission from livestock housing reduce the size of the TAN pool in manure storage.

Volatilisation of NH3 from the application of inorganic N fertil- isers were calculated using fixed emission factors and sub-divided into those applied to grassland and arable land (Hyde et al., 2003). The emission factors used for applications to grassland were 23%, 1.6%, and 1.6% of the applied N for urea, calcium ammonium ni- trate (CAN) and other inorganic N applications, respectively, and were assumed to be 50% less for applications to arable land.

cattle on pasture, spreading of inorganic fertilisers, indirectly fol- lowing nitrate leaching and run-off, and following re-deposition of volatilised gases to soils and waters (IPCC, 2006). Emissions of N20 from stored liquid and solid manure were estimated using the emission factors 0.01 g N2O-N/m³ per day and 0.4 N2O-N/ m³ per day, respectively (Sneath et al., 2006).

After subtraction of gaseous N losses during housing, manure storage, and manure spreading, N20 emission from the application of manure to land was estimated. Nitrous oxide emission for slurry spreading in autumn or winter was estimated as 1.10% of available N applied (Chadwick et al., 2011). However, the N20 emission fac- tor for spring or summer application was 0.51% of available N ap- plied; because crops are growing during this period creating a bigger N sink (Chadwick et al., 2011). An emission factor of 0.2% was used to estimate N20 emission from the application of solid manure (Chadwick et al., 2011). The global default estimate factor for N20 from manure deposited by grazing animals is 2% of N ex- creted (IPCC, 2006). However, research conducted in temperate re- gions estimates that 1% of N deposited during grazing is typically emitted as N20 (Galbally et al., 2010; Van Groenigen et al., 2005). Thus, based on the location of farms in this study it was estimated that 1% of N deposited during grazing is emitted as N20. Emission of N20 from the application of artificial fertiliser was estimated as 1% of N applied (IPCC, 2006).

Indirect N20 following re-deposition of NH3 to soil and water was calculated as 1% of the total NH3 volatilised on-farm (IPCC, 2006). Nitrous oxide emission from nitrate leached was calculated as 0.75% of total N leached on-farm (IPCC, 2006). On-farm emis- sions of NO were estimated as 21% of N20 emissions (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). The equation includes direct NO from fertilisers and the soil. Potential leaching of N and P to water was computed by means of a farm gate balance approach. According to this ap- proach, N and P available for leaching and surface run-off was quantified as total inputs minus the N or P removed in product minus the N lost directly to the atmosphere. This indicator repre- sents the N or P surplus (Oenema et al., 2005). The fraction of N lost to water was estimated as 10% of the N surplus, which was based on research studies and a national monitoring program measuring N loss to waterways (del Prado et al., 2006; Neut, 1999). The P sur- plus lost to waterways was estimated as 0.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was between 1 and 10 kg, 1.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus was between 10 and 20 kg, and 2.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was > 20 kg (Schulte et al., 2010).

The data collected and estimated for on-farm resource use were used to quantify environmental impacts associated with purchased inputs. The emissions associated with the manufacture of these external inputs were linearly related to the amount used on-farm, e.g. off-farm CO2 emission from fertiliser production is dependant

on the amount and type of fertiliser used on-farm (Van der Werf et al., 2009). Data on the environmental impact associated with the production and supply of these inputs were based on the Eco- invent database (Ecoinvent, 2010) and data from the literature, e.g. Howley et al. (2009). The key inputs purchased on-farm along with the resources used and pollutants released during their manufac- ture are presented in Table 4.

Greenhouse gas emission associated with concentrate produc- tion in Table 4 also includes direct emissions from the conversion of rainforest to agricultural land. Soybean from Brazil and palm fruit bunches from Malaysia were the main concentrate compo- nents responsible for GHG emission from land use change (Ecoin- vent, 2010). The average land use change emission from soybean and palm fruit were estimated by firstly quantifying the total land use change emission caused by soybean and palm cultivation (Jungbluth et al., 2007). The average GHG emissions for soybean and palm fruit from land use change were estimated by dividing the total GHG emission from land use change by the total crop area. This resulted in estimates of 2.6 and 5.5 tonnes of CO2/ha for land use change GHG emission from soybean and palm fruit production, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2010).
24: <header>2.2.2. Life cycle inventory </header>

In the inventory analysis stage, materials used and pollutants related to each production process were quantified. The LCA model built within Simapro (Pré Consultants, 2008) was used during this stage to quantify on-farm and off-farm resource use and pollutants. Data collected on farm by Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009) were predominately used to determine the quantity of materials used directly on farm, for instance the amount of fertiliser used. The work of Rice and Quinlan (2003) was used to estimate farm energy use for grassland management, fertiliser spreading, grass silage production and slurry spreading. The report of Nemecek and Kägi (2007) was used to estimate energy required for the production of silage other than grass. The review of Dalgaard et al. (2001) was used to estimate energy consumption for the loading and handling, together with the subsequent distri- bution of feedstuffs.

On-farm, carbon is assimilated or emitted as carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4). Potential sinks of carbon are soil carbon sequestration and pastoral photosynthesis. However, the model as- sumes that no assimilation of carbon occurs. Carbon sequestration was excluded because most pastures within Ireland are semi or permanent pasture. Therefore, based on the IPCC (2006), the car- bon flux from the soil is most likely to be in equilibrium. Pastoral photosynthesis was not considered because these simulations are based on productive grasslands where carbon fixing through pho- tosynthesis would approximate that of carbon oxidation through senescence and decay. Emissions of CO2 were limited to fuel com- bustion, lime and urea application as the carbon balance of soil was assumed to be in equilibrium. Diesel is the main fuel used on-farm. Carbon dioxide from this source was estimated as 2.62 kg CO2/1 diesel (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Diesel combustion also emits other gases including sulphur dioxide (SO2), NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx) and nitrous oxide (N20) and uses finite energy. Emissions of these gases were estimated using data from Nemecek and Kägi (2007) and finite energy use was estimated as 36.4 MJ/1 of diesel (Howley et al., 2009). Carbon dioxide from lime and urea use was estimated as 0.12 kg CO2-C/kg of lime and 0.2 kg CO2-C/kg of urea, respectively (IPCC, 2006).

Methane emission arises from feed digestion (enteric fermenta- tion) and manure management. Fixed emission factors that esti- mate that 6.5% of gross energy intake is lost as CH4 (MJ/day) were used to estimate enteric CH4 from cows when grazed grass was the major dietary component for the dairy cow (>50% of DMI; O'Mara, 2006) and for all dietary intakes for 1-2-year old heifers and calves (IPCC, 2006). When grass was not the major die- tary component of the cow, the following Mitscherlich algorithm from Mills et al. (2003) was used:
CH4 (MJ/day) = CH4 max min) exp kn(-0.0011x(ADF/Starch)+0.0045)xMEI (1)
where CH4 max is the maximum possible enteric CH4 emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), CH4 min is the minimum possible enteric CH4

emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), MEI is the metabolisable energy intake (MJ/day) and ADF is acid detergent fibre. This method of computing CH4 from enteric fermentation requires data on animal weight, body weight change, animal activity, feed digestibility, milk production and composition, and replacement rate of cows. The MDSM was linked to the LCA model to obtain this information. Combining the MDSM with the LCA model provided data on animal and feed variables and was used to compute feed intakes according to the net energy required for milk production, maintenance and body weight change (Jarrige, 1989). These feed variables were also used with the digestibility of feed to estimate animal manure pro- duction. The quantity of manure requiring storage was calculated based on the number of days animals spent housed.

Methane emission from manure management arises during storage and following application on land. Methane is also released from manure deposited by animals on pasture but the quantities are negligible (IPCC, 2006), and as a result this source was ex- cluded. Methane from stored liquid manure and solid manure (>15% DM) was estimated as 22 g CH4-C/m³ per day and 5.9 g CH4-C/m³ per day, respectively (Husted, 1994; Sneath et al., 2006). Methane from stored silage effluent was estimated as 0.165 kg CH4/106 biological oxygen demand (US EPA, 1990). The season dependent emission factor of Chadwick et al. (2000) was used for quantifying methane emission from manure spreading. Methane from slurry spreading in spring, summer and autumn was estimated using the emission factors 12 g CH4/m3 0.07 g CH4/m3 and 6.8 g CH4/m3, respectively.

Nitrogen emissions from animals were derived following the quantification of N excreted. Nitrogen excreted from animals was estimated as the difference between the total N intake (calculated as the dietary DM intake and the N content of the diet) and the N output in product (meat, milk). The equations of Kebreab et al. (2001) were used to separate N excretion between faeces and ur- line. The quantity of N stored was estimated based on the time the animal spent indoors. Ammonia, NO, N20 and NO were the main nitrogen emissions quantified.
On-farm, NH3 volatilises during grazing, housing, manure stor- age and during the application of organic and inorganic fertilisers.

The mass flow model of Webb and Misselbrook (2004) was adopted to quantify NH3 emission related to manure. This ap- proach estimates NH3 from a pool of total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) in livestock excreta where the TAN pool is estimated from the annual N excreted by ruminants, the proportion of annual N excreted in the form of TAN (60%; Webb, 2001) and the number of livestock. The TAN pool is then partitioned between manure deposited in housing and on pasture based on the length of the grazing season. Total ammoniacal nitrogen deposited in animal housing is tracked through the different manure management stages (housing, storage, spreading). Estimates are made for each stage, with emission factors from Table 3. Emission expressed as g NH3-N/UK livestock unit (luuk) per day (a UK livestock unit is de- fined as 500 kg bodyweight) were converted to a proportion of TAN excreted using the following equation from Webb and Misselbrook (2004): EF%TAN = (EFluuk/1000) X (Y/500) (100/TAN) (2)

where EF%TAN is the emission factor expressed as a percentage of the TAN pool, is the emission factor in g NH3-N/luuk per day, Y is the mean bodyweight per animal (kg), d is number of housing days and TAN is the annual TAN excretion per animal (kg). Ammonia emission quantified at each stage was subtracted from the TAN pool to determine TAN available for the next stage, e.g. NH3 emission from livestock housing reduce the size of the TAN pool in manure storage.

Volatilisation of NH3 from the application of inorganic N fertil- isers were calculated using fixed emission factors and sub-divided into those applied to grassland and arable land (Hyde et al., 2003). The emission factors used for applications to grassland were 23%, 1.6%, and 1.6% of the applied N for urea, calcium ammonium ni- trate (CAN) and other inorganic N applications, respectively, and were assumed to be 50% less for applications to arable land.

Nitrous oxide is produced as an intermediate product in the denitrification process or as a by-product in the nitrification pro- cess. During the denitrification process NO may also be emitted. Nitrous oxide emissions generated on-farm occurs directly from manure storage and application, manure deposited by grazing

Nitrous oxide emission from nitrate leached was calculated as 0.75% of total N leached on-farm (IPCC, 2006). On-farm emis- sions of NO were estimated as 21% of N20 emissions (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). The equation includes direct NO from fertilisers and the soil. Potential leaching of N and P to water was computed by means of a farm gate balance approach. According to this ap- proach, N and P available for leaching and surface run-off was quantified as total inputs minus the N or P removed in product minus the N lost directly to the atmosphere. This indicator repre- sents the N or P surplus (Oenema et al., 2005). The fraction of N lost to water was estimated as 10% of the N surplus, which was based on research studies and a national monitoring program measuring N loss to waterways (del Prado et al., 2006; Neut, 1999). The P sur- plus lost to waterways was estimated as 0.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was between 1 and 10 kg, 1.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus was between 10 and 20 kg, and 2.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was > 20 kg (Schulte et al., 2010).

The data collected and estimated for on-farm resource use were used to quantify environmental impacts associated with purchased inputs. The emissions associated with the manufacture of these external inputs were linearly related to the amount used on-farm, e.g. off-farm CO2 emission from fertiliser production is dependant

on the amount and type of fertiliser used on-farm (Van der Werf et al., 2009). Data on the environmental impact associated with the production and supply of these inputs were based on the Eco- invent database (Ecoinvent, 2010) and data from the literature, e.g. Howley et al. (2009). The key inputs purchased on-farm along with the resources used and pollutants released during their manufac- ture are presented in Table 4.

Greenhouse gas emission associated with concentrate produc- tion in Table 4 also includes direct emissions from the conversion of rainforest to agricultural land. Soybean from Brazil and palm fruit bunches from Malaysia were the main concentrate compo- nents responsible for GHG emission from land use change (Ecoin- vent, 2010). The average land use change emission from soybean and palm fruit were estimated by firstly quantifying the total land use change emission caused by soybean and palm cultivation (Jungbluth et al., 2007). The average GHG emissions for soybean and palm fruit from land use change were estimated by dividing the total GHG emission from land use change by the total crop area. This resulted in estimates of 2.6 and 5.5 tonnes of CO2/ha for land use change GHG emission from soybean and palm fruit production, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2010).
26: <header>2.2.2. Life cycle inventory </header>

In the inventory analysis stage, materials used and pollutants related to each production process were quantified. The LCA model built within Simapro (Pré Consultants, 2008) was used during this stage to quantify on-farm and off-farm resource use and pollutants. Data collected on farm by Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009) were predominately used to determine the quantity of materials used directly on farm, for instance the amount of fertiliser used. The work of Rice and Quinlan (2003) was used to estimate farm energy use for grassland management, fertiliser spreading, grass silage production and slurry spreading. The report of Nemecek and Kägi (2007) was used to estimate energy required for the production of silage other than grass. The review of Dalgaard et al. (2001) was used to estimate energy consumption for the loading and handling, together with the subsequent distri- bution of feedstuffs.

On-farm, carbon is assimilated or emitted as carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4). Potential sinks of carbon are soil carbon sequestration and pastoral photosynthesis. However, the model as- sumes that no assimilation of carbon occurs. Carbon sequestration was excluded because most pastures within Ireland are semi or permanent pasture. Therefore, based on the IPCC (2006), the car- bon flux from the soil is most likely to be in equilibrium. Pastoral photosynthesis was not considered because these simulations are based on productive grasslands where carbon fixing through pho- tosynthesis would approximate that of carbon oxidation through senescence and decay. Emissions of CO2 were limited to fuel com- bustion, lime and urea application as the carbon balance of soil was assumed to be in equilibrium. Diesel is the main fuel used on-farm. Carbon dioxide from this source was estimated as 2.62 kg CO2/1 diesel (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Diesel combustion also emits other gases including sulphur dioxide (SO2), NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx) and nitrous oxide (N20) and uses finite energy. Emissions of these gases were estimated using data from Nemecek and Kägi (2007) and finite energy use was estimated as 36.4 MJ/1 of diesel (Howley et al., 2009). Carbon dioxide from lime and urea use was estimated as 0.12 kg CO2-C/kg of lime and 0.2 kg CO2-C/kg of urea, respectively (IPCC, 2006).

Methane emission arises from feed digestion (enteric fermenta- tion) and manure management. Fixed emission factors that esti- mate that 6.5% of gross energy intake is lost as CH4 (MJ/day) were used to estimate enteric CH4 from cows when grazed grass was the major dietary component for the dairy cow (>50% of DMI; O'Mara, 2006) and for all dietary intakes for 1-2-year old heifers and calves (IPCC, 2006). When grass was not the major die- tary component of the cow, the following Mitscherlich algorithm from Mills et al. (2003) was used:
CH4 (MJ/day) = CH4 max min) exp kn(-0.0011x(ADF/Starch)+0.0045)xMEI (1)
where CH4 max is the maximum possible enteric CH4 emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), CH4 min is the minimum possible enteric CH4

emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), MEI is the metabolisable energy intake (MJ/day) and ADF is acid detergent fibre. This method of computing CH4 from enteric fermentation requires data on animal weight, body weight change, animal activity, feed digestibility, milk production and composition, and replacement rate of cows. The MDSM was linked to the LCA model to obtain this information. Combining the MDSM with the LCA model provided data on animal and feed variables and was used to compute feed intakes according to the net energy required for milk production, maintenance and body weight change (Jarrige, 1989). These feed variables were also used with the digestibility of feed to estimate animal manure pro- duction. The quantity of manure requiring storage was calculated based on the number of days animals spent housed.

Methane emission from manure management arises during storage and following application on land. Methane is also released from manure deposited by animals on pasture but the quantities are negligible (IPCC, 2006), and as a result this source was ex- cluded. Methane from stored liquid manure and solid manure (>15% DM) was estimated as 22 g CH4-C/m³ per day and 5.9 g CH4-C/m³ per day, respectively (Husted, 1994; Sneath et al., 2006). Methane from stored silage effluent was estimated as 0.165 kg CH4/106 biological oxygen demand (US EPA, 1990). The season dependent emission factor of Chadwick et al. (2000) was used for quantifying methane emission from manure spreading. Methane from slurry spreading in spring, summer and autumn was estimated using the emission factors 12 g CH4/m3 0.07 g CH4/m3 and 6.8 g CH4/m3, respectively.

Nitrogen emissions from animals were derived following the quantification of N excreted. Nitrogen excreted from animals was estimated as the difference between the total N intake (calculated as the dietary DM intake and the N content of the diet) and the N output in product (meat, milk). The equations of Kebreab et al. (2001) were used to separate N excretion between faeces and ur- line. The quantity of N stored was estimated based on the time the animal spent indoors. Ammonia, NO, N20 and NO were the main nitrogen emissions quantified.
On-farm, NH3 volatilises during grazing, housing, manure stor- age and during the application of organic and inorganic fertilisers.

The mass flow model of Webb and Misselbrook (2004) was adopted to quantify NH3 emission related to manure. This ap- proach estimates NH3 from a pool of total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) in livestock excreta where the TAN pool is estimated from the annual N excreted by ruminants, the proportion of annual N excreted in the form of TAN (60%; Webb, 2001) and the number of livestock. The TAN pool is then partitioned between manure deposited in housing and on pasture based on the length of the grazing season. Total ammoniacal nitrogen deposited in animal housing is tracked through the different manure management stages (housing, storage, spreading). Estimates are made for each stage, with emission factors from Table 3. Emission expressed as g NH3-N/UK livestock unit (luuk) per day (a UK livestock unit is de- fined as 500 kg bodyweight) were converted to a proportion of TAN excreted using the following equation from Webb and Misselbrook (2004): EF%TAN = (EFluuk/1000) X (Y/500) (100/TAN) (2)

where EF%TAN is the emission factor expressed as a percentage of the TAN pool, is the emission factor in g NH3-N/luuk per day, Y is the mean bodyweight per animal (kg), d is number of housing days and TAN is the annual TAN excretion per animal (kg). Ammonia emission quantified at each stage was subtracted from the TAN pool to determine TAN available for the next stage, e.g. NH3 emission from livestock housing reduce the size of the TAN pool in manure storage.

Volatilisation of NH3 from the application of inorganic N fertil- isers were calculated using fixed emission factors and sub-divided into those applied to grassland and arable land (Hyde et al., 2003). The emission factors used for applications to grassland were 23%, 1.6%, and 1.6% of the applied N for urea, calcium ammonium ni- trate (CAN) and other inorganic N applications, respectively, and were assumed to be 50% less for applications to arable land.

Nitrous oxide is produced as an intermediate product in the denitrification process or as a by-product in the nitrification pro- cess. During the denitrification process NO may also be emitted. Nitrous oxide emissions generated on-farm occurs directly from manure storage and application, manure deposited by grazing

cattle on pasture, spreading of inorganic fertilisers, indirectly fol- lowing nitrate leaching and run-off, and following re-deposition of volatilised gases to soils and waters (IPCC, 2006). (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009) were predominately used to determine the quantity of materials used directly on farm, for instance the amount of fertiliser used. The work of Rice and Quinlan (2003) was used to estimate farm energy use for grassland management, fertiliser spreading, grass silage production and slurry spreading. The report of Nemecek and Kägi (2007) was used to estimate energy required for the production of silage other than grass. The review of Dalgaard et al. (2001) was used to estimate energy consumption for the loading and handling, together with the subsequent distri- bution of feedstuffs.

On-farm, carbon is assimilated or emitted as carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4). Potential sinks of carbon are soil carbon sequestration and pastoral photosynthesis. However, the model as- sumes that no assimilation of carbon occurs. Carbon sequestration was excluded because most pastures within Ireland are semi or permanent pasture. Therefore, based on the IPCC (2006), the car- bon flux from the soil is most likely to be in equilibrium. Pastoral photosynthesis was not considered because these simulations are based on productive grasslands where carbon fixing through pho- tosynthesis would approximate that of carbon oxidation through senescence and decay. Emissions of CO2 were limited to fuel com- bustion, lime and urea application as the carbon balance of soil was assumed to be in equilibrium. Diesel is the main fuel used on-farm. Carbon dioxide from this source was estimated as 2.62 kg CO2/1 diesel (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Diesel combustion also emits other gases including sulphur dioxide (SO2), NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx) and nitrous oxide (N20) and uses finite energy. Emissions of these gases were estimated using data from Nemecek and Kägi (2007) and finite energy use was estimated as 36.4 MJ/1 of diesel (Howley et al., 2009). Carbon dioxide from lime and urea use was estimated as 0.12 kg CO2-C/kg of lime and 0.2 kg CO2-C/kg of urea, respectively (IPCC, 2006).

Methane emission arises from feed digestion (enteric fermenta- tion) and manure management. Fixed emission factors that esti- mate that 6.5% of gross energy intake is lost as CH4 (MJ/day) were used to estimate enteric CH4 from cows when grazed grass was the major dietary component for the dairy cow (>50% of DMI; O'Mara, 2006) and for all dietary intakes for 1-2-year old heifers and calves (IPCC, 2006). When grass was not the major die- tary component of the cow, the following Mitscherlich algorithm from Mills et al. (2003) was used:
CH4 (MJ/day) = CH4 max min) exp kn(-0.0011x(ADF/Starch)+0.0045)xMEI (1)
where CH4 max is the maximum possible enteric CH4 emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), CH4 min is the minimum possible enteric CH4

emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), MEI is the metabolisable energy intake (MJ/day) and ADF is acid detergent fibre. This method of computing CH4 from enteric fermentation requires data on animal weight, body weight change, animal activity, feed digestibility, milk production and composition, and replacement rate of cows. The MDSM was linked to the LCA model to obtain this information. Combining the MDSM with the LCA model provided data on animal and feed variables and was used to compute feed intakes according to the net energy required for milk production, maintenance and body weight change (Jarrige, 1989). These feed variables were also used with the digestibility of feed to estimate animal manure pro- duction. The quantity of manure requiring storage was calculated based on the number of days animals spent housed.

Methane emission from manure management arises during storage and following application on land. Methane is also released from manure deposited by animals on pasture but the quantities are negligible (IPCC, 2006), and as a result this source was ex- cluded. Methane from stored liquid manure and solid manure (>15% DM) was estimated as 22 g CH4-C/m³ per day and 5.9 g CH4-C/m³ per day, respectively (Husted, 1994; Sneath et al., 2006). Methane from stored silage effluent was estimated as 0.165 kg CH4/106 biological oxygen demand (US EPA, 1990). The season dependent emission factor of Chadwick et al. (2000) was used for quantifying methane emission from manure spreading. Methane from slurry spreading in spring, summer and autumn was estimated using the emission factors 12 g CH4/m3 0.07 g CH4/m3 and 6.8 g CH4/m3, respectively.

Nitrogen emissions from animals were derived following the quantification of N excreted. Nitrogen excreted from animals was estimated as the difference between the total N intake (calculated as the dietary DM intake and the N content of the diet) and the N output in product (meat, milk). The equations of Kebreab et al. (2001) were used to separate N excretion between faeces and ur- line. The quantity of N stored was estimated based on the time the animal spent indoors. Ammonia, NO, N20 and NO were the main nitrogen emissions quantified.
On-farm, NH3 volatilises during grazing, housing, manure stor- age and during the application of organic and inorganic fertilisers.

The mass flow model of Webb and Misselbrook (2004) was adopted to quantify NH3 emission related to manure. This ap- proach estimates NH3 from a pool of total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) in livestock excreta where the TAN pool is estimated from the annual N excreted by ruminants, the proportion of annual N excreted in the form of TAN (60%; Webb, 2001) and the number of livestock. The TAN pool is then partitioned between manure deposited in housing and on pasture based on the length of the grazing season. Total ammoniacal nitrogen deposited in animal housing is tracked through the different manure management stages (housing, storage, spreading). Estimates are made for each stage, with emission factors from Table 3. Emission expressed as g NH3-N/UK livestock unit (luuk) per day (a UK livestock unit is de- fined as 500 kg bodyweight) were converted to a proportion of TAN excreted using the following equation from Webb and Misselbrook (2004): EF%TAN = (EFluuk/1000) X (Y/500) (100/TAN) (2)

where EF%TAN is the emission factor expressed as a percentage of the TAN pool, is the emission factor in g NH3-N/luuk per day, Y is the mean bodyweight per animal (kg), d is number of housing days and TAN is the annual TAN excretion per animal (kg). Ammonia emission quantified at each stage was subtracted from the TAN pool to determine TAN available for the next stage, e.g. NH3 emission from livestock housing reduce the size of the TAN pool in manure storage.

Volatilisation of NH3 from the application of inorganic N fertil- isers were calculated using fixed emission factors and sub-divided into those applied to grassland and arable land (Hyde et al., 2003). The emission factors used for applications to grassland were 23%, 1.6%, and 1.6% of the applied N for urea, calcium ammonium ni- trate (CAN) and other inorganic N applications, respectively, and were assumed to be 50% less for applications to arable land.

Nitrous oxide is produced as an intermediate product in the denitrification process or as a by-product in the nitrification pro- cess. During the denitrification process NO may also be emitted. Nitrous oxide emissions generated on-farm occurs directly from manure storage and application, manure deposited by grazing

cattle on pasture, spreading of inorganic fertilisers, indirectly fol- lowing nitrate leaching and run-off, and following re-deposition of volatilised gases to soils and waters (IPCC, 2006). Emissions of N20 from stored liquid and solid manure were estimated using the emission factors 0.01 g N2O-N/m³ per day and 0.4 N2O-N/ m³ per day, respectively (Sneath et al., 2006).

After subtraction of gaseous N losses during housing, manure storage, and manure spreading, N20 emission from the application of manure to land was estimated. Nitrous oxide emission for slurry spreading in autumn or winter was estimated as 1.10% of available N applied (Chadwick et al., 2011). However, the N20 emission fac- tor for spring or summer application was 0.51% of available N ap- plied; because crops are growing during this period creating a bigger N sink (Chadwick et al., 2011). An emission factor of 0.2% was used to estimate N20 emission from the application of solid manure (Chadwick et al., 2011). The global default estimate factor for N20 from manure deposited by grazing animals is 2% of N ex- creted (IPCC, 2006). However, research conducted in temperate re- gions estimates that 1% of N deposited during grazing is typically emitted as N20 (Galbally et al., 2010; Van Groenigen et al., 2005). Thus, based on the location of farms in this study it was estimated that 1% of N deposited during grazing is emitted as N20. Emission of N20 from the application of artificial fertiliser was estimated as 1% of N applied (IPCC, 2006).

Indirect N20 following re-deposition of NH3 to soil and water was calculated as 1% of the total NH3 volatilised on-farm (IPCC, 2006). Nitrous oxide emission from nitrate leached was calculated as 0.75% of total N leached on-farm (IPCC, 2006). On-farm emis- sions of NO were estimated as 21% of N20 emissions (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). The equation includes direct NO from fertilisers and the soil. Potential leaching of N and P to water was computed by means of a farm gate balance approach. According to this ap- proach, N and P available for leaching and surface run-off was quantified as total inputs minus the N or P removed in product minus the N lost directly to the atmosphere. This indicator repre- sents the N or P surplus (Oenema et al., 2005). The fraction of N lost to water was estimated as 10% of the N surplus, which was based on research studies and a national monitoring program measuring N loss to waterways (del Prado et al., 2006; Neut, 1999). The P sur- plus lost to waterways was estimated as 0.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was between 1 and 10 kg, 1.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus was between 10 and 20 kg, and 2.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was > 20 kg (Schulte et al., 2010).

The data collected and estimated for on-farm resource use were used to quantify environmental impacts associated with purchased inputs. The emissions associated with the manufacture of these external inputs were linearly related to the amount used on-farm, e.g. off-farm CO2 emission from fertiliser production is dependant

on the amount and type of fertiliser used on-farm (Van der Werf et al., 2009). Data on the environmental impact associated with the production and supply of these inputs were based on the Eco- invent database (Ecoinvent, 2010) and data from the literature, e.g. Howley et al. (2009). The key inputs purchased on-farm along with the resources used and pollutants released during their manufac- ture are presented in Table 4.

Greenhouse gas emission associated with concentrate produc- tion in Table 4 also includes direct emissions from the conversion of rainforest to agricultural land. Soybean from Brazil and palm fruit bunches from Malaysia were the main concentrate compo- nents responsible for GHG emission from land use change (Ecoin- vent, 2010). The average land use change emission from soybean and palm fruit were estimated by firstly quantifying the total land use change emission caused by soybean and palm cultivation (Jungbluth et al., 2007). The average GHG emissions for soybean and palm fruit from land use change were estimated by dividing the total GHG emission from land use change by the total crop area. This resulted in estimates of 2.6 and 5.5 tonnes of CO2/ha for land use change GHG emission from soybean and palm fruit production, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2010).
