Methane is also released from manure deposited by animals on pasture but the quantities are negligible (IPCC, 2006), and as a result this source was ex- cluded. Methane from stored liquid manure and solid manure (>15% DM) was estimated as 22 g CH4-C/m³ per day and 5.9 g CH4-C/m³ per day, respectively (Husted, 1994; Sneath et al., 2006). Methane from stored silage effluent was estimated as 0.165 kg CH4/106 biological oxygen demand (US EPA, 1990). The season dependent emission factor of Chadwick et al. (2000) was used for quantifying methane emission from manure spreading. Methane from slurry spreading in spring, summer and autumn was estimated using the emission factors 12 g CH4/m3 0.07 g CH4/m3 and 6.8 g CH4/m3, respectively.

Nitrogen emissions from animals were derived following the quantification of N excreted. Nitrogen excreted from animals was estimated as the difference between the total N intake (calculated as the dietary DM intake and the N content of the diet) and the N output in product (meat, milk). The equations of Kebreab et al. (2001) were used to separate N excretion between faeces and ur- line. The quantity of N stored was estimated based on the time the animal spent indoors. Ammonia, NO, N20 and NO were the main nitrogen emissions quantified.
On-farm, NH3 volatilises during grazing, housing, manure stor- age and during the application of organic and inorganic fertilisers.

The mass flow model of Webb and Misselbrook (2004) was adopted to quantify NH3 emission related to manure. This ap- proach estimates NH3 from a pool of total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) in livestock excreta where the TAN pool is estimated from the annual N excreted by ruminants, the proportion of annual N excreted in the form of TAN (60%; Webb, 2001) and the number of livestock. The TAN pool is then partitioned between manure deposited in housing and on pasture based on the length of the grazing season. Total ammoniacal nitrogen deposited in animal housing is tracked through the different manure management stages (housing, storage, spreading). Estimates are made for each stage, with emission factors from Table 3. Emission expressed as g NH3-N/UK livestock unit (luuk) per day (a UK livestock unit is de- fined as 500 kg bodyweight) were converted to a proportion of TAN excreted using the following equation from Webb and Misselbrook (2004): EF%TAN = (EFluuk/1000) X (Y/500) (100/TAN) (2)

where EF%TAN is the emission factor expressed as a percentage of the TAN pool, is the emission factor in g NH3-N/luuk per day, Y is the mean bodyweight per animal (kg), d is number of housing days and TAN is the annual TAN excretion per animal (kg). Ammonia emission quantified at each stage was subtracted from the TAN pool to determine TAN available for the next stage, e.g. NH3 emission from livestock housing reduce the size of the TAN pool in manure storage.

Volatilisation of NH3 from the application of inorganic N fertil- isers were calculated using fixed emission factors and sub-divided into those applied to grassland and arable land (Hyde et al., 2003). The emission factors used for applications to grassland were 23%, 1.6%, and 1.6% of the applied N for urea, calcium ammonium ni- trate (CAN) and other inorganic N applications, respectively, and were assumed to be 50% less for applications to arable land.

Nitrous oxide is produced as an intermediate product in the denitrification process or as a by-product in the nitrification pro- cess. During the denitrification process NO may also be emitted. Nitrous oxide emissions generated on-farm occurs directly from manure storage and application, manure deposited by grazing

cattle on pasture, spreading of inorganic fertilisers, indirectly fol- lowing nitrate leaching and run-off, and following re-deposition of volatilised gases to soils and waters (IPCC, 2006). Emissions of N20 from stored liquid and solid manure were estimated using the emission factors 0.01 g N2O-N/m³ per day and 0.4 N2O-N/ m³ per day, respectively (Sneath et al., 2006).

After subtraction of gaseous N losses during housing, manure storage, and manure spreading, N20 emission from the application of manure to land was estimated. Nitrous oxide emission for slurry spreading in autumn or winter was estimated as 1.10% of available N applied (Chadwick et al., 2011). However, the N20 emission fac- tor for spring or summer application was 0.51% of available N ap- plied; because crops are growing during this period creating a bigger N sink (Chadwick et al., 2011). An emission factor of 0.2% was used to estimate N20 emission from the application of solid manure (Chadwick et al., 2011). The global default estimate factor for N20 from manure deposited by grazing animals is 2% of N ex- creted (IPCC, 2006). However, research conducted in temperate re- gions estimates that 1% of N deposited during grazing is typically emitted as N20 (Galbally et al., 2010; Van Groenigen et al., 2005). Thus, based on the location of farms in this study it was estimated that 1% of N deposited during grazing is emitted as N20. Emission of N20 from the application of artificial fertiliser was estimated as 1% of N applied (IPCC, 2006).

Indirect N20 following re-deposition of NH3 to soil and water was calculated as 1% of the total NH3 volatilised on-farm (IPCC, 2006). Nitrous oxide emission from nitrate leached was calculated as 0.75% of total N leached on-farm (IPCC, 2006). On-farm emis- sions of NO were estimated as 21% of N20 emissions (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). The equation includes direct NO from fertilisers and the soil. Potential leaching of N and P to water was computed by means of a farm gate balance approach. According to this ap- proach, N and P available for leaching and surface run-off was quantified as total inputs minus the N or P removed in product minus the N lost directly to the atmosphere. This indicator repre- sents the N or P surplus (Oenema et al., 2005). The fraction of N lost to water was estimated as 10% of the N surplus, which was based on research studies and a national monitoring program measuring N loss to waterways (del Prado et al., 2006; Neut, 1999). The P sur- plus lost to waterways was estimated as 0.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was between 1 and 10 kg, 1.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus was between 10 and 20 kg, and 2.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was > 20 kg (Schulte et al., 2010).

The data collected and estimated for on-farm resource use were used to quantify environmental impacts associated with purchased inputs. The emissions associated with the manufacture of these external inputs were linearly related to the amount used on-farm, e.g. off-farm CO2 emission from fertiliser production is dependant

on the amount and type of fertiliser used on-farm (Van der Werf et al., 2009). Data on the environmental impact associated with the production and supply of these inputs were based on the Eco- invent database (Ecoinvent, 2010) and data from the literature, e.g. Howley et al. (2009). The key inputs purchased on-farm along with the resources used and pollutants released during their manufac- ture are presented in Table 4.

Greenhouse gas emission associated with concentrate produc- tion in Table 4 also includes direct emissions from the conversion of rainforest to agricultural land. Soybean from Brazil and palm fruit bunches from Malaysia were the main concentrate compo- nents responsible for GHG emission from land use change (Ecoin- vent, 2010). The average land use change emission from soybean and palm fruit were estimated by firstly quantifying the total land use change emission caused by soybean and palm cultivation (Jungbluth et al., 2007). The average GHG emissions for soybean and palm fruit from land use change were estimated by dividing the total GHG emission from land use change by the total crop area. This resulted in estimates of 2.6 and 5.5 tonnes of CO2/ha for land use change GHG emission from soybean and palm fruit production, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2010).
31: <header>2.2.2. Life cycle inventory </header>

In the inventory analysis stage, materials used and pollutants related to each production process were quantified. The LCA model built within Simapro (Pré Consultants, 2008) was used during this stage to quantify on-farm and off-farm resource use and pollutants. Data collected on farm by Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009) were predominately used to determine the quantity of materials used directly on farm, for instance the amount of fertiliser used. The work of Rice and Quinlan (2003) was used to estimate farm energy use for grassland management, fertiliser spreading, grass silage production and slurry spreading. The report of Nemecek and Kägi (2007) was used to estimate energy required for the production of silage other than grass. The review of Dalgaard et al. (2001) was used to estimate energy consumption for the loading and handling, together with the subsequent distri- bution of feedstuffs.

On-farm, carbon is assimilated or emitted as carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4). Potential sinks of carbon are soil carbon sequestration and pastoral photosynthesis. However, the model as- sumes that no assimilation of carbon occurs. Carbon sequestration was excluded because most pastures within Ireland are semi or permanent pasture. Therefore, based on the IPCC (2006), the car- bon flux from the soil is most likely to be in equilibrium. Pastoral photosynthesis was not considered because these simulations are based on productive grasslands where carbon fixing through pho- tosynthesis would approximate that of carbon oxidation through senescence and decay. Emissions of CO2 were limited to fuel com- bustion, lime and urea application as the carbon balance of soil was assumed to be in equilibrium. Diesel is the main fuel used on-farm. Carbon dioxide from this source was estimated as 2.62 kg CO2/1 diesel (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Diesel combustion also emits other gases including sulphur dioxide (SO2), NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx) and nitrous oxide (N20) and uses finite energy. Emissions of these gases were estimated using data from Nemecek and Kägi (2007) and finite energy use was estimated as 36.4 MJ/1 of diesel (Howley et al., 2009). Carbon dioxide from lime and urea use was estimated as 0.12 kg CO2-C/kg of lime and 0.2 kg CO2-C/kg of urea, respectively (IPCC, 2006).

Methane emission arises from feed digestion (enteric fermenta- tion) and manure management. Fixed emission factors that esti- mate that 6.5% of gross energy intake is lost as CH4 (MJ/day) were used to estimate enteric CH4 from cows when grazed grass was the major dietary component for the dairy cow (>50% of DMI; O'Mara, 2006) and for all dietary intakes for 1-2-year old heifers and calves (IPCC, 2006). When grass was not the major die- tary component of the cow, the following Mitscherlich algorithm from Mills et al. (2003) was used:
CH4 (MJ/day) = CH4 max min) exp kn(-0.0011x(ADF/Starch)+0.0045)xMEI (1)
where CH4 max is the maximum possible enteric CH4 emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), CH4 min is the minimum possible enteric CH4

emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), MEI is the metabolisable energy intake (MJ/day) and ADF is acid detergent fibre. This method of computing CH4 from enteric fermentation requires data on animal weight, body weight change, animal activity, feed digestibility, milk production and composition, and replacement rate of cows. The MDSM was linked to the LCA model to obtain this information. Combining the MDSM with the LCA model provided data on animal and feed variables and was used to compute feed intakes according to the net energy required for milk production, maintenance and body weight change (Jarrige, 1989). These feed variables were also used with the digestibility of feed to estimate animal manure pro- duction. The quantity of manure requiring storage was calculated based on the number of days animals spent housed.

Methane emission from manure management arises during storage and following application on land. Methane is also released from manure deposited by animals on pasture but the quantities are negligible (IPCC, 2006), and as a result this source was ex- cluded. Methane from stored liquid manure and solid manure (>15% DM) was estimated as 22 g CH4-C/m³ per day and 5.9 g CH4-C/m³ per day, respectively (Husted, 1994; Sneath et al., 2006). Methane from stored silage effluent was estimated as 0.165 kg CH4/106 biological oxygen demand (US EPA, 1990). The season dependent emission factor of Chadwick et al. (2000) was used for quantifying methane emission from manure spreading. Methane from slurry spreading in spring, summer and autumn was estimated using the emission factors 12 g CH4/m3 0.07 g CH4/m3 and 6.8 g CH4/m3, respectively.

Nitrogen emissions from animals were derived following the quantification of N excreted. Nitrogen excreted from animals was estimated as the difference between the total N intake (calculated as the dietary DM intake and the N content of the diet) and the N output in product (meat, milk). The equations of Kebreab et al. (2001) were used to separate N excretion between faeces and ur- line. The quantity of N stored was estimated based on the time the animal spent indoors. Ammonia, NO, N20 and NO were the main nitrogen emissions quantified.

where EF%TAN is the emission factor expressed as a percentage of the TAN pool, is the emission factor in g NH3-N/luuk per day, Y is the mean bodyweight per animal (kg), d is number of housing days and TAN is the annual TAN excretion per animal (kg). Ammonia emission quantified at each stage was subtracted from the TAN pool to determine TAN available for the next stage, e.g. NH3 emission from livestock housing reduce the size of the TAN pool in manure storage.

Volatilisation of NH3 from the application of inorganic N fertil- isers were calculated using fixed emission factors and sub-divided into those applied to grassland and arable land (Hyde et al., 2003). Nitrous oxide is produced as an intermediate product in the denitrification process or as a by-product in the nitrification pro- cess. During the denitrification process NO may also be emitted. Nitrous oxide emissions generated on-farm occurs directly from manure storage and application, manure deposited by grazing

cattle on pasture, spreading of inorganic fertilisers, indirectly fol- lowing nitrate leaching and run-off, and following re-deposition of volatilised gases to soils and waters (IPCC, 2006). Emissions of N20 from stored liquid and solid manure were estimated using the emission factors 0.01 g N2O-N/m³ per day and 0.4 N2O-N/ m³ per day, respectively (Sneath et al., 2006).

After subtraction of gaseous N losses during housing, manure storage, and manure spreading, N20 emission from the application of manure to land was estimated. Nitrous oxide emission for slurry spreading in autumn or winter was estimated as 1.10% of available N applied (Chadwick et al., 2011). However, the N20 emission fac- tor for spring or summer application was 0.51% of available N ap- plied; because crops are growing during this period creating a bigger N sink (Chadwick et al., 2011). An emission factor of 0.2% was used to estimate N20 emission from the application of solid manure (Chadwick et al., 2011). The global default estimate factor for N20 from manure deposited by grazing animals is 2% of N ex- creted (IPCC, 2006). However, research conducted in temperate re- gions estimates that 1% of N deposited during grazing is typically emitted as N20 (Galbally et al., 2010; Van Groenigen et al., 2005). Thus, based on the location of farms in this study it was estimated that 1% of N deposited during grazing is emitted as N20. Emission of N20 from the application of artificial fertiliser was estimated as 1% of N applied (IPCC, 2006).

Indirect N20 following re-deposition of NH3 to soil and water was calculated as 1% of the total NH3 volatilised on-farm (IPCC, 2006). Nitrous oxide emission from nitrate leached was calculated as 0.75% of total N leached on-farm (IPCC, 2006). On-farm emis- sions of NO were estimated as 21% of N20 emissions (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). The equation includes direct NO from fertilisers and the soil. Potential leaching of N and P to water was computed by means of a farm gate balance approach. According to this ap- proach, N and P available for leaching and surface run-off was quantified as total inputs minus the N or P removed in product minus the N lost directly to the atmosphere. This indicator repre- sents the N or P surplus (Oenema et al., 2005). The fraction of N lost to water was estimated as 10% of the N surplus, which was based on research studies and a national monitoring program measuring N loss to waterways (del Prado et al., 2006; Neut, 1999). The P sur- plus lost to waterways was estimated as 0.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was between 1 and 10 kg, 1.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus was between 10 and 20 kg, and 2.5 kg P/ha per year when the P surplus/ha was > 20 kg (Schulte et al., 2010).

The data collected and estimated for on-farm resource use were used to quantify environmental impacts associated with purchased inputs. The emissions associated with the manufacture of these external inputs were linearly related to the amount used on-farm, e.g. off-farm CO2 emission from fertiliser production is dependant

on the amount and type of fertiliser used on-farm (Van der Werf et al., 2009). Data on the environmental impact associated with the production and supply of these inputs were based on the Eco- invent database (Ecoinvent, 2010) and data from the literature, e.g. Howley et al. (2009). The key inputs purchased on-farm along with the resources used and pollutants released during their manufac- ture are presented in Table 4.

Greenhouse gas emission associated with concentrate produc- tion in Table 4 also includes direct emissions from the conversion of rainforest to agricultural land. Soybean from Brazil and palm fruit bunches from Malaysia were the main concentrate compo- nents responsible for GHG emission from land use change (Ecoin- vent, 2010). The average land use change emission from soybean and palm fruit were estimated by firstly quantifying the total land use change emission caused by soybean and palm cultivation (Jungbluth et al., 2007). The average GHG emissions for soybean and palm fruit from land use change were estimated by dividing the total GHG emission from land use change by the total crop area. This resulted in estimates of 2.6 and 5.5 tonnes of CO2/ha for land use change GHG emission from soybean and palm fruit production, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2010).
33: <header>2.2.2. Life cycle inventory </header>

In the inventory analysis stage, materials used and pollutants related to each production process were quantified. The LCA model built within Simapro (Pré Consultants, 2008) was used during this stage to quantify on-farm and off-farm resource use and pollutants. Data collected on farm by Patton et al. (2009) and Olmos et al. (2009) were predominately used to determine the quantity of materials used directly on farm, for instance the amount of fertiliser used. The work of Rice and Quinlan (2003) was used to estimate farm energy use for grassland management, fertiliser spreading, grass silage production and slurry spreading. The report of Nemecek and Kägi (2007) was used to estimate energy required for the production of silage other than grass. The review of Dalgaard et al. (2001) was used to estimate energy consumption for the loading and handling, together with the subsequent distri- bution of feedstuffs.

On-farm, carbon is assimilated or emitted as carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4). Potential sinks of carbon are soil carbon sequestration and pastoral photosynthesis. However, the model as- sumes that no assimilation of carbon occurs. Carbon sequestration was excluded because most pastures within Ireland are semi or permanent pasture. Therefore, based on the IPCC (2006), the car- bon flux from the soil is most likely to be in equilibrium. Pastoral photosynthesis was not considered because these simulations are based on productive grasslands where carbon fixing through pho- tosynthesis would approximate that of carbon oxidation through senescence and decay. Emissions of CO2 were limited to fuel com- bustion, lime and urea application as the carbon balance of soil was assumed to be in equilibrium. Diesel is the main fuel used on-farm. Carbon dioxide from this source was estimated as 2.62 kg CO2/1 diesel (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Diesel combustion also emits other gases including sulphur dioxide (SO2), NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx) and nitrous oxide (N20) and uses finite energy. Emissions of these gases were estimated using data from Nemecek and Kägi (2007) and finite energy use was estimated as 36.4 MJ/1 of diesel (Howley et al., 2009). Carbon dioxide from lime and urea use was estimated as 0.12 kg CO2-C/kg of lime and 0.2 kg CO2-C/kg of urea, respectively (IPCC, 2006).

Methane emission arises from feed digestion (enteric fermenta- tion) and manure management. Fixed emission factors that esti- mate that 6.5% of gross energy intake is lost as CH4 (MJ/day) were used to estimate enteric CH4 from cows when grazed grass was the major dietary component for the dairy cow (>50% of DMI; O'Mara, 2006) and for all dietary intakes for 1-2-year old heifers and calves (IPCC, 2006). When grass was not the major die- tary component of the cow, the following Mitscherlich algorithm from Mills et al. (2003) was used:
CH4 (MJ/day) = CH4 max min) exp kn(-0.0011x(ADF/Starch)+0.0045)xMEI (1)
where CH4 max is the maximum possible enteric CH4 emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), CH4 min is the minimum possible enteric CH4

emission (MJ CH4/animal per day), MEI is the metabolisable energy intake (MJ/day) and ADF is acid detergent fibre. This method of computing CH4 from enteric fermentation requires data on animal weight, body weight change, animal activity, feed digestibility, milk production and composition, and replacement rate of cows. The MDSM was linked to the LCA model to obtain this information. Combining the MDSM with the LCA model provided data on animal and feed variables and was used to compute feed intakes according to the net energy required for milk production, maintenance and body weight change (Jarrige, 1989). These feed variables were also used with the digestibility of feed to estimate animal manure pro- duction. The quantity of manure requiring storage was calculated based on the number of days animals spent housed.

Methane emission from manure management arises during storage and following application on land. Methane is also released from manure deposited by animals on pasture but the quantities are negligible (IPCC, 2006), and as a result this source was ex- cluded. Methane from stored liquid manure and solid manure (>15% DM) was estimated as 22 g CH4-C/m³ per day and 5.9 g CH4-C/m³ per day, respectively (Husted, 1994; Sneath et al., 2006). Methane from stored silage effluent was estimated as 0.165 kg CH4/106 biological oxygen demand (US EPA, 1990). The season dependent emission factor of Chadwick et al. (2000) was used for quantifying methane emission from manure spreading. Methane from slurry spreading in spring, summer and autumn was estimated using the emission factors 12 g CH4/m3 0.07 g CH4/m3 and 6.8 g CH4/m3, respectively.

Nitrogen emissions from animals were derived following the quantification of N excreted. Nitrogen excreted from animals was estimated as the difference between the total N intake (calculated as the dietary DM intake and the N content of the diet) and the N output in product (meat, milk). The equations of Kebreab et al. (2001) were used to separate N excretion between faeces and ur- line. The quantity of N stored was estimated based on the time the animal spent indoors. Ammonia, NO, N20 and NO were the main nitrogen emissions quantified.
On-farm, NH3 volatilises during grazing, housing, manure stor- age and during the application of organic and inorganic fertilisers.

The mass flow model of Webb and Misselbrook (2004) was adopted to quantify NH3 emission related to manure. This ap- proach estimates NH3 from a pool of total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) in livestock excreta where the TAN pool is estimated from the annual N excreted by ruminants, the proportion of annual N excreted in the form of TAN (60%; Webb, 2001) and the number of livestock. The TAN pool is then partitioned between manure deposited in housing and on pasture based on the length of the grazing season. Total ammoniacal nitrogen deposited in animal housing is tracked through the different manure management stages (housing, storage, spreading). Estimates are made for each stage, with emission factors from Table 3. Emission expressed as g NH3-N/UK livestock unit (luuk) per day (a UK livestock unit is de- fined as 500 kg bodyweight) were converted to a proportion of TAN excreted using the following equation from Webb and Misselbrook (2004): EF%TAN = (EFluuk/1000) X (Y/500) (100/TAN) (2)

where EF%TAN is the emission factor expressed as a percentage of the TAN pool, is the emission factor in g NH3-N/luuk per day, Y is the mean bodyweight per animal (kg), d is number of housing days and TAN is the annual TAN excretion per animal (kg). Ammonia emission quantified at each stage was subtracted from the TAN pool to determine TAN available for the next stage, e.g. NH3 emission from livestock housing reduce the size of the TAN pool in manure storage.

Volatilisation of NH3 from the application of inorganic N fertil- isers were calculated using fixed emission factors and sub-divided into those applied to grassland and arable land (Hyde et al., 2003). The emission factors used for applications to grassland were 23%, 1.6%, and 1.6% of the applied N for urea, calcium ammonium ni- trate (CAN) and other inorganic N applications, respectively, and were assumed to be 50% less for applications to arable land.

Nitrous oxide is produced as an intermediate product in the denitrification process or as a by-product in the nitrification pro- cess. During the denitrification process NO may also be emitted. Nitrous oxide emissions generated on-farm occurs directly from manure storage and application, manure deposited by grazing

34: Resources used and pollutants computed during the inventory analysis are translated into environmental impacts during this stage. The environmental impacts selected, substances included and characterisation factors are presented in Table 5. Eutrophica- tion covers all potential impacts of high environmental levels of macronutrients, particularly N and P (Guinee et al., 2002). Global warming potential (GWP) is the impact anthropogenic greenhouse gases have on heat radiation absorption of the atmosphere. Acidifi- cation is the effect acidifying pollutants have on soil, groundwater, surface waters, biological organisms and buildings (Heijungs et al., 1992). Non-renewable energy use refers to the depletion of finite energy resources and was calculated using the lower heating values proposed in the cumulative energy demand method in Simapro (Pré Consultants, 2008), crude oil: 42.6 MJ/kg, natural gas: 35 MJ/m³, uranium: 451,000 MJ/kg, coal: 18 MJ/kg, lignite: 8 MJ/kg. Land use refers to the loss of land as a resource, in the sense of being tempo- rarily unavailable for other purposes due to the growing of crops.
35: The results of LCA studies are affected by the parameters used to predict each environmental impact and assumptions made during the modelling process (ISO, 2006a). Parameters and assumptions that could strongly influence the final outcomes were analysed in this study using a sensitivity analysis. Scenario modelling was adopted to complete the analysis, because it is useful in cases where

The LCA model assumes the carbon balance of permanent grass- land is in a steady state. However, studies have reported that per- manent grassland can be an important carbon sink (Jones and Donnelly, 2004). Thus, based on the review of Soussana et al. (2010), it was estimated that Irish permanent grassland seques- tered 2.5 t C/ha per annum in S1. The choice of allocation procedure is controversial in LCA studies, mainly due to its potentially large influence on results (Weidema, 2000). The effect of using different allocation rules for milk and meat and concentrate ingredients was assessed in S2. In this scenario, economic allocation rather than biological causal allocation was used for milk and meat. Mass-based allocation rather than economic allocation was used in S2 for concentrate ingredients (Table 2). Soybean meal is an important contributor to impacts of dairy systems (Gerber et al., 2010). In
S3, the effect of substituting soybean meal with a similar protein meal, rape meal, and using soybean from a different country (USA) were examined with data from Ecoinvent (Ecoinvent, 2010).

The sensitivity of environmental impacts to enteric fermenta- tion (S4) was assessed with measurements of enteric CH4 from dairy cows used in this study. O'Neill et al. (2011) measured enteric CH4 emission from Holstein-Friesian cows consuming either grass or TMR during late spring (April) and early summer (May). The study found that grass-fed Holstein-Friesian cows emitted less CH4 per unit of intake (18.1 g CH4/kg of DMI) than TMR-fed cows (20.3 g CH4/kg of DMI). These data were used in S4. The facility used to store manure has a significant effect on gaseous fluxes (Hyde et al., 2003; IPCC, 2006). In S5, the effect of storing manure in solid systems was analysed using emission factors from the pre- vious section. The S6 scenario assessed the effect of using the glo- bal default emission factor, 2% of N excreted (IPCC, 2006), to estimate N20 from manure deposited by grazing animals.

The LCA model assumes the carbon balance of permanent grass- land is in a steady state. However, studies have reported that per- manent grassland can be an important carbon sink (Jones and Donnelly, 2004). Thus, based on the review of Soussana et al. (2010), it was estimated that Irish permanent grassland seques- tered 2.5 t C/ha per annum in S1. The choice of allocation procedure is controversial in LCA studies, mainly due to its potentially large influence on results (Weidema, 2000). The effect of using different allocation rules for milk and meat and concentrate ingredients was assessed in S2. In this scenario, economic allocation rather than biological causal allocation was used for milk and meat. Mass-based allocation rather than economic allocation was used in S2 for concentrate ingredients (Table 2). Soybean meal is an important contributor to impacts of dairy systems (Gerber et al., 2010). In
S3, the effect of substituting soybean meal with a similar protein meal, rape meal, and using soybean from a different country (USA) were examined with data from Ecoinvent (Ecoinvent, 2010).

The sensitivity of environmental impacts to enteric fermenta- tion (S4) was assessed with measurements of enteric CH4 from dairy cows used in this study. O'Neill et al. (2011) measured enteric CH4 emission from Holstein-Friesian cows consuming either grass or TMR during late spring (April) and early summer (May). The study found that grass-fed Holstein-Friesian cows emitted less CH4 per unit of intake (18.1 g CH4/kg of DMI) than TMR-fed cows (20.3 g CH4/kg of DMI). These data were used in S4. The facility used to store manure has a significant effect on gaseous fluxes (Hyde et al., 2003; IPCC, 2006). In S5, the effect of storing manure in solid systems was analysed using emission factors from the pre- vious section. The S6 scenario assessed the effect of using the glo- bal default emission factor, 2% of N excreted (IPCC, 2006), to estimate N20 from manure deposited by grazing animals.

systems. The results are given by environmental impact and quan- tified per tonne of FPCM, per tonne of MS, per on-farm area (hect- ares) and per total farm area (on-farm and off-farm area). The results show that total (on-farm and off-farm) GHG emission (in GWP) per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS was on average 15.5% less for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system. Similarly, per on-farm area the grass-based system re- duced on-farm GHG emission relative to the confinement system (50%), but increased total GHG emission (10%) when estimated per total farm area. Methane accounted for 52% of total GHG emis- sion in the grass-based and for 49% in the confinement system, N20 accounted for 31% in the grass-based system and for 16% in the confinement system, CO2 accounted for 17% in the grass-based sys- tem and 35% in the confinement system.

Table 7 shows that in the grass-based system total GHG emis- sion consisted mainly of CH4 emission from enteric fermentation (46%); CO2 and N20 associated with the production of fertiliser (14.5%), N20 emission from artificial fertiliser application (10%)

and N20 from excreta deposited during grazing (8%). Enteric fer- mentation was the greatest contributor to on-farm GHG emission (60%) followed by fertiliser application (12.5%) and N20 from ex- creta deposited during grazing (10%). On average, dairy cows, heif- ers (1-2 year old) and calves (0-1 year) emitted 124, 52 and 25 kg of enteric CH4/head per year, respectively, in the grass-based sys- tem. Off-farm GHG emission consisted mainly of N20 and CO2 emissions from the manufacture of fertiliser (61%) and GHG emis- sions associated with the production of electricity and lime (20%). Total GHG emission from feed production was predominately de- rived from forage in the grass-based system (42%; Table 7). The main contributors to total GHG emission in the confine- ment system were CH4 emission from enteric fermentation (36%) and manure storage (12%), GHG emissions from electricity and fer- tiliser production (14.5%), and CO2 emissions from land use change (11%) and fuel burned during processing, transportation and agri- cultural field operations (7.5%; Table 8). On-farm GHG emission in the confinement system were mainly due to enteric CH4 (64%).

a NO and NO2. b Non-renewable energy use per MJ. C Global warming potential per kg of CO2 equivalent assuming a 100-year time horizon. d Acidification potential per kg of SO2 equivalent. e Eutrophication potential per kg of PO4 equivalent. f Electricity used for on-farm animal operations, e.g. milking. g Manure deposited by grazing cattle on pasture. h Fuel burned during transport, agricultural field operations and processing of concentrate ingredients. i Phosphorous and phosphate loss. j Emissions and resources used during processing of concentrate ingredients excluding pollutants from fuel combustion and electricity generation.
Table 8 Contribution analysis (source percentage of pollutant, resource and environmental impact e.g. global warming potential) of a confinement dairy farm.

a NO and NO2. b Non-renewable energy use per MJ. C Global warming potential per kg of CO2 equivalent assuming a 100-year time horizon. d Acidification potential per kg of SO2 equivalent. e Eutrophication potental per kg of PO4 equivalent. f Electricity used for on-farm animal operations, e.g. milking. g Fuel burned during transport, agricultural field operations and processing of concentrate ingredients. h Phosphorous and phosphate loss. i Manure deposited by grazing cattle on pasture. j Emissions and resources used during processing of concentrate ingredients excluding pollutants from fuel combustion and electricity generation.

cows in the confinement system was 8.5% lower (20.6 enteric CH4/kg DMI) relative to cows in the grass-based system (22.5 g en- teric CH4/kg DMI). The main sources of off-farm GHG emission were CO2 from land use change (25%) and fuel combustion (14%), GHG emissions from electricity (22%) and fertiliser (10%) produc- tion, and indirect N20 from NO3 leaching (7%). Total GHG emission from the production of feed in the confinement system was pre- dominately derived from concentrate (32%; Table 8).

Total non-renewable energy use was on average 42% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area and per total farm area, total non-renewable energy use was greatest for the confinement system and least for the grass-based dairy farm. For both systems, the majority of non- renewable energy was consumed during the production of fuel, electricity and fertiliser, ranging from 68% for the confinement sys- tem to 79% for the grass-based system (Tables 7 and 8). In the case of the grass-based system, fertiliser production was the greatest consumer of energy (46%) followed by electricity generation (33%) and fuel combustion (21%). Electricity production was the largest consumer of energy in the confinement system (55.5%) fol- lowed by fuel combustion (31%).

Total land use per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS was on average 23% less for the grass-based system relative to the confine- ment system (Table 6). For the confinement system, 57% of total land use was for the off-farm production of concentrate ingredients (Table 8). In the case of the grass-based system, 11% of total land use was used to produce concentrate (Table 7).

Table 6 shows that total acidification for the grass-based system was on average 42% lower than the confinement system when esti- mated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS. On-farm acidificat- ion for the grass-based system per on-farm area was 71% lower than the confinement system, but for total acidification per total farm area the reduction was 25%. Ammonia accounted for 89% of total acidification in the grass-based system and for 87% in the con- finement system. Table 7 shows that in the grass-based dairy farm, the main contributor to total acidification was NH3 volatilised dur- ing manure application (39.5%), followed by NH3 emitted from cat- tle housing and manure storage (24.5%), and NH3 emitted from excreta deposited by grazing cattle (14%). Manure storage and spreading was the main cause of on-farm acidification. Fertiliser production (57%), electricity generation (16.5%) and fuel combus- tion (13%) were the main sources of off-farm acidification in the grass-based system.

The main contributors to total acidification in the confinement system were NH3 released during cattle housing and manure stor- age (56%) and NH3 released during manure spreading (24%; Table 8). All on-farm acidification was related to storage and use of or- ganic and inorganic fertilisers. Off-farm acidification consisted mainly of NH3 emission from the application of manure to maize silage and concentrate ingredients (36%), emissions from fuel com- bustion (21%) and emissions from electricity (16%) and fertiliser (8.5%) production. In both dairy systems, the contribution of forage production to total acidification was greater than that of concen- trate production (Tables 7 and 8).

Total eutrophication for the grass-based system was on average 72% of the eutrophication for the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area, on-farm eutrophication was 50% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system, but for total eutrophication per total farm area the reduction was 6% (Table 6). In the grass-based system, NO3, NH3, NOx PO and P accounted for 51%, 40%, 3%, 5.5% and 0.5% of total eutrophication, respectively. In the confinement system, NO, NH3, NOx, PO and P accounted for 35%, 49%, 4%, 10% and 2% of total eutrophica- tion, respectively. The main contributor to total eutrophication in

the grass-based system was NO3, PO and P loss during the pro- duction of feed (54%; Table 7). Nitrate, PO and P losses were also the main source of on-farm eutrophication for the grass-based sys- tem (55%) followed by NH3 volatilisation from manure spreading (20%), cattle housing and manure storage (13%), and excreta depos- ited during grazing (7%). Fertiliser production (44%) and NO, PO and P loss from concentrate production (42%) were the main contributors to off-farm eutrophication in the grass-based system. Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the grass-based system (82%; Table 7).

The largest contributor to total eutrophication for the confine- ment system was NO3, PO and P loss during feed production (44%; Table 8), followed by NH3 volatilised during cattle housing and manure storage (32%). Ammonia lost during cattle housing and manure storage (59%) were the main sources of on-farm eutro- phication for the confinement system. Off-farm eutrophication was mainly caused by NO3, PO and P loss during the production of maize silage and concentrate (68%), and NH3 emission from man- ure spreading (12%). Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the confinement system (36%; Table 8).

Per on-farm area, on-farm eutrophication was 50% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system, but for total eutrophication per total farm area the reduction was 6% (Table 6). In the grass-based system, NO3, NH3, NOx PO and P accounted for 51%, 40%, 3%, 5.5% and 0.5% of total eutrophication, respectively. In the confinement system, NO, NH3, NOx, PO and P accounted for 35%, 49%, 4%, 10% and 2% of total eutrophica- tion, respectively. The main contributor to total eutrophication in

the grass-based system was NO3, PO and P loss during the pro- duction of feed (54%; Table 7). Fertiliser production (44%) and NO, PO and P loss from concentrate production (42%) were the main contributors to off-farm eutrophication in the grass-based system. Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the grass-based system (82%; Table 7).

The largest contributor to total eutrophication for the confine- ment system was NO3, PO and P loss during feed production (44%; Table 8), followed by NH3 volatilised during cattle housing and manure storage (32%). Ammonia lost during cattle housing and manure storage (59%) were the main sources of on-farm eutro- phication for the confinement system. Off-farm eutrophication was mainly caused by NO3, PO and P loss during the production of maize silage and concentrate (68%), and NH3 emission from man- ure spreading (12%). Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the confinement system (36%; Table 8).

For the confinement system, 57% of total land use was for the off-farm production of concentrate ingredients (Table 8). In the case of the grass-based system, 11% of total land use was used to produce concentrate (Table 7).

Table 6 shows that total acidification for the grass-based system was on average 42% lower than the confinement system when esti- mated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS. On-farm acidificat- ion for the grass-based system per on-farm area was 71% lower than the confinement system, but for total acidification per total farm area the reduction was 25%. Ammonia accounted for 89% of total acidification in the grass-based system and for 87% in the con- finement system. Table 7 shows that in the grass-based dairy farm, the main contributor to total acidification was NH3 volatilised dur- ing manure application (39.5%), followed by NH3 emitted from cat- tle housing and manure storage (24.5%), and NH3 emitted from excreta deposited by grazing cattle (14%). Manure storage and spreading was the main cause of on-farm acidification. Fertiliser production (57%), electricity generation (16.5%) and fuel combus- tion (13%) were the main sources of off-farm acidification in the grass-based system.

The main contributors to total acidification in the confinement system were NH3 released during cattle housing and manure stor- age (56%) and NH3 released during manure spreading (24%; Table 8). All on-farm acidification was related to storage and use of or- ganic and inorganic fertilisers. Off-farm acidification consisted mainly of NH3 emission from the application of manure to maize silage and concentrate ingredients (36%), emissions from fuel com- bustion (21%) and emissions from electricity (16%) and fertiliser (8.5%) production. In both dairy systems, the contribution of forage production to total acidification was greater than that of concen- trate production (Tables 7 and 8).

Total eutrophication for the grass-based system was on average 72% of the eutrophication for the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area, on-farm eutrophication was 50% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system, but for total eutrophication per total farm area the reduction was 6% (Table 6). In the grass-based system, NO3, NH3, NOx PO and P accounted for 51%, 40%, 3%, 5.5% and 0.5% of total eutrophication, respectively. In the confinement system, NO, NH3, NOx, PO and P accounted for 35%, 49%, 4%, 10% and 2% of total eutrophica- tion, respectively. The main contributor to total eutrophication in

the grass-based system was NO3, PO and P loss during the pro- duction of feed (54%; Table 7). Nitrate, PO and P losses were also the main source of on-farm eutrophication for the grass-based sys- tem (55%) followed by NH3 volatilisation from manure spreading (20%), cattle housing and manure storage (13%), and excreta depos- ited during grazing (7%). Fertiliser production (44%) and NO, PO and P loss from concentrate production (42%) were the main contributors to off-farm eutrophication in the grass-based system. Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the grass-based system (82%; Table 7).

The largest contributor to total eutrophication for the confine- ment system was NO3, PO and P loss during feed production (44%; Table 8), followed by NH3 volatilised during cattle housing and manure storage (32%). Ammonia lost during cattle housing and manure storage (59%) were the main sources of on-farm eutro- phication for the confinement system. Off-farm eutrophication was mainly caused by NO3, PO and P loss during the production of maize silage and concentrate (68%), and NH3 emission from man- ure spreading (12%). Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the confinement system (36%; Table 8).

C Global warming potential per kg of CO2 equivalent assuming a 100-year time horizon. d Acidification potential per kg of SO2 equivalent. e Eutrophication potental per kg of PO4 equivalent. f Electricity used for on-farm animal operations, e.g. milking. g Fuel burned during transport, agricultural field operations and processing of concentrate ingredients. h Phosphorous and phosphate loss. i Manure deposited by grazing cattle on pasture. j Emissions and resources used during processing of concentrate ingredients excluding pollutants from fuel combustion and electricity generation.

cows in the confinement system was 8.5% lower (20.6 enteric CH4/kg DMI) relative to cows in the grass-based system (22.5 g en- teric CH4/kg DMI). The main sources of off-farm GHG emission were CO2 from land use change (25%) and fuel combustion (14%), GHG emissions from electricity (22%) and fertiliser (10%) produc- tion, and indirect N20 from NO3 leaching (7%). Total GHG emission from the production of feed in the confinement system was pre- dominately derived from concentrate (32%; Table 8).

Total non-renewable energy use was on average 42% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area and per total farm area, total non-renewable energy use was greatest for the confinement system and least for the grass-based dairy farm. For both systems, the majority of non- renewable energy was consumed during the production of fuel, electricity and fertiliser, ranging from 68% for the confinement sys- tem to 79% for the grass-based system (Tables 7 and 8). In the case of the grass-based system, fertiliser production was the greatest consumer of energy (46%) followed by electricity generation (33%) and fuel combustion (21%). Electricity production was the largest consumer of energy in the confinement system (55.5%) fol- lowed by fuel combustion (31%).

Total land use per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS was on average 23% less for the grass-based system relative to the confine- ment system (Table 6). For the confinement system, 57% of total land use was for the off-farm production of concentrate ingredients (Table 8). In the case of the grass-based system, 11% of total land use was used to produce concentrate (Table 7).

Table 6 shows that total acidification for the grass-based system was on average 42% lower than the confinement system when esti- mated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS. On-farm acidificat- ion for the grass-based system per on-farm area was 71% lower than the confinement system, but for total acidification per total farm area the reduction was 25%. Ammonia accounted for 89% of total acidification in the grass-based system and for 87% in the con- finement system. Table 7 shows that in the grass-based dairy farm, the main contributor to total acidification was NH3 volatilised dur- ing manure application (39.5%), followed by NH3 emitted from cat- tle housing and manure storage (24.5%), and NH3 emitted from excreta deposited by grazing cattle (14%). Manure storage and spreading was the main cause of on-farm acidification. Fertiliser production (57%), electricity generation (16.5%) and fuel combus- tion (13%) were the main sources of off-farm acidification in the grass-based system.

The main contributors to total acidification in the confinement system were NH3 released during cattle housing and manure stor- age (56%) and NH3 released during manure spreading (24%; Table 8). All on-farm acidification was related to storage and use of or- ganic and inorganic fertilisers. Off-farm acidification consisted mainly of NH3 emission from the application of manure to maize silage and concentrate ingredients (36%), emissions from fuel com- bustion (21%) and emissions from electricity (16%) and fertiliser (8.5%) production. In both dairy systems, the contribution of forage production to total acidification was greater than that of concen- trate production (Tables 7 and 8).

Total eutrophication for the grass-based system was on average 72% of the eutrophication for the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area, on-farm eutrophication was 50% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system, but for total eutrophication per total farm area the reduction was 6% (Table 6). In the grass-based system, NO3, NH3, NOx PO and P accounted for 51%, 40%, 3%, 5.5% and 0.5% of total eutrophication, respectively. In the confinement system, NO, NH3, NOx, PO and P accounted for 35%, 49%, 4%, 10% and 2% of total eutrophica- tion, respectively. The main contributor to total eutrophication in

the grass-based system was NO3, PO and P loss during the pro- duction of feed (54%; Table 7). Nitrate, PO and P losses were also the main source of on-farm eutrophication for the grass-based sys- tem (55%) followed by NH3 volatilisation from manure spreading (20%), cattle housing and manure storage (13%), and excreta depos- ited during grazing (7%). Fertiliser production (44%) and NO, PO and P loss from concentrate production (42%) were the main contributors to off-farm eutrophication in the grass-based system. Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the grass-based system (82%; Table 7).

The largest contributor to total eutrophication for the confine- ment system was NO3, PO and P loss during feed production (44%; Table 8), followed by NH3 volatilised during cattle housing and manure storage (32%). Ammonia lost during cattle housing and manure storage (59%) were the main sources of on-farm eutro- phication for the confinement system. Off-farm eutrophication was mainly caused by NO3, PO and P loss during the production of maize silage and concentrate (68%), and NH3 emission from man- ure spreading (12%). Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the confinement system (36%; Table 8).

Nitrate, PO and P losses were also the main source of on-farm eutrophication for the grass-based sys- tem (55%) followed by NH3 volatilisation from manure spreading (20%), cattle housing and manure storage (13%), and excreta depos- ited during grazing (7%). Fertiliser production (44%) and NO, PO and P loss from concentrate production (42%) were the main contributors to off-farm eutrophication in the grass-based system. Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the grass-based system (82%; Table 7).

The largest contributor to total eutrophication for the confine- ment system was NO3, PO and P loss during feed production (44%; Table 8), followed by NH3 volatilised during cattle housing and manure storage (32%). Ammonia lost during cattle housing and manure storage (59%) were the main sources of on-farm eutro- phication for the confinement system. Off-farm eutrophication was mainly caused by NO3, PO and P loss during the production of maize silage and concentrate (68%), and NH3 emission from man- ure spreading (12%). Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the confinement system (36%; Table 8).

cows in the confinement system was 8.5% lower (20.6 enteric CH4/kg DMI) relative to cows in the grass-based system (22.5 g en- teric CH4/kg DMI). The main sources of off-farm GHG emission were CO2 from land use change (25%) and fuel combustion (14%), GHG emissions from electricity (22%) and fertiliser (10%) produc- tion, and indirect N20 from NO3 leaching (7%). Total GHG emission from the production of feed in the confinement system was pre- dominately derived from concentrate (32%; Table 8).

Total non-renewable energy use was on average 42% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area and per total farm area, total non-renewable energy use was greatest for the confinement system and least for the grass-based dairy farm. For both systems, the majority of non- renewable energy was consumed during the production of fuel, electricity and fertiliser, ranging from 68% for the confinement sys- tem to 79% for the grass-based system (Tables 7 and 8). In the case of the grass-based system, fertiliser production was the greatest consumer of energy (46%) followed by electricity generation (33%) and fuel combustion (21%). Electricity production was the largest consumer of energy in the confinement system (55.5%) fol- lowed by fuel combustion (31%).

Total land use per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS was on average 23% less for the grass-based system relative to the confine- ment system (Table 6). In the case of the grass-based system, 11% of total land use was used to produce concentrate (Table 7).

Table 6 shows that total acidification for the grass-based system was on average 42% lower than the confinement system when esti- mated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS. On-farm acidificat- ion for the grass-based system per on-farm area was 71% lower than the confinement system, but for total acidification per total farm area the reduction was 25%. Ammonia accounted for 89% of total acidification in the grass-based system and for 87% in the con- finement system. Table 7 shows that in the grass-based dairy farm, the main contributor to total acidification was NH3 volatilised dur- ing manure application (39.5%), followed by NH3 emitted from cat- tle housing and manure storage (24.5%), and NH3 emitted from excreta deposited by grazing cattle (14%). The main contributors to total acidification in the confinement system were NH3 released during cattle housing and manure stor- age (56%) and NH3 released during manure spreading (24%; Table 8). All on-farm acidification was related to storage and use of or- ganic and inorganic fertilisers. Off-farm acidification consisted mainly of NH3 emission from the application of manure to maize silage and concentrate ingredients (36%), emissions from fuel com- bustion (21%) and emissions from electricity (16%) and fertiliser (8.5%) production. In both dairy systems, the contribution of forage production to total acidification was greater than that of concen- trate production (Tables 7 and 8).

In the grass-based system, NO3, NH3, NOx PO and P accounted for 51%, 40%, 3%, 5.5% and 0.5% of total eutrophication, respectively. In the confinement system, NO, NH3, NOx, PO and P accounted for 35%, 49%, 4%, 10% and 2% of total eutrophica- tion, respectively. The main contributor to total eutrophication in

the grass-based system was NO3, PO and P loss during the pro- duction of feed (54%; Table 7). Table 7 shows that in the grass-based dairy farm, the main contributor to total acidification was NH3 volatilised dur- ing manure application (39.5%), followed by NH3 emitted from cat- tle housing and manure storage (24.5%), and NH3 emitted from excreta deposited by grazing cattle (14%). Manure storage and spreading was the main cause of on-farm acidification. Fertiliser production (57%), electricity generation (16.5%) and fuel combus- tion (13%) were the main sources of off-farm acidification in the grass-based system.

The main contributors to total acidification in the confinement system were NH3 released during cattle housing and manure stor- age (56%) and NH3 released during manure spreading (24%; Table 8). All on-farm acidification was related to storage and use of or- ganic and inorganic fertilisers. Off-farm acidification consisted mainly of NH3 emission from the application of manure to maize silage and concentrate ingredients (36%), emissions from fuel com- bustion (21%) and emissions from electricity (16%) and fertiliser (8.5%) production. In both dairy systems, the contribution of forage production to total acidification was greater than that of concen- trate production (Tables 7 and 8).

Total eutrophication for the grass-based system was on average 72% of the eutrophication for the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area, on-farm eutrophication was 50% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system, but for total eutrophication per total farm area the reduction was 6% (Table 6). In the grass-based system, NO3, NH3, NOx PO and P accounted for 51%, 40%, 3%, 5.5% and 0.5% of total eutrophication, respectively. In the confinement system, NO, NH3, NOx, PO and P accounted for 35%, 49%, 4%, 10% and 2% of total eutrophica- tion, respectively. The main contributor to total eutrophication in

the grass-based system was NO3, PO and P loss during the pro- duction of feed (54%; Table 7). Nitrate, PO and P losses were also the main source of on-farm eutrophication for the grass-based sys- tem (55%) followed by NH3 volatilisation from manure spreading (20%), cattle housing and manure storage (13%), and excreta depos- ited during grazing (7%). Fertiliser production (44%) and NO, PO and P loss from concentrate production (42%) were the main contributors to off-farm eutrophication in the grass-based system. Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the grass-based system (82%; Table 7).

The largest contributor to total eutrophication for the confine- ment system was NO3, PO and P loss during feed production (44%; Table 8), followed by NH3 volatilised during cattle housing and manure storage (32%). Ammonia lost during cattle housing and manure storage (59%) were the main sources of on-farm eutro- phication for the confinement system. Off-farm eutrophication was mainly caused by NO3, PO and P loss during the production of maize silage and concentrate (68%), and NH3 emission from man- ure spreading (12%). Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the confinement system (36%; Table 8).

The main sources of off-farm GHG emission were CO2 from land use change (25%) and fuel combustion (14%), GHG emissions from electricity (22%) and fertiliser (10%) produc- tion, and indirect N20 from NO3 leaching (7%). Total GHG emission from the production of feed in the confinement system was pre- dominately derived from concentrate (32%; Table 8).

Total non-renewable energy use was on average 42% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area and per total farm area, total non-renewable energy use was greatest for the confinement system and least for the grass-based dairy farm. For both systems, the majority of non- renewable energy was consumed during the production of fuel, electricity and fertiliser, ranging from 68% for the confinement sys- tem to 79% for the grass-based system (Tables 7 and 8). In the case of the grass-based system, fertiliser production was the greatest consumer of energy (46%) followed by electricity generation (33%) and fuel combustion (21%). Electricity production was the largest consumer of energy in the confinement system (55.5%) fol- lowed by fuel combustion (31%).

Total land use per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS was on average 23% less for the grass-based system relative to the confine- ment system (Table 6). For the confinement system, 57% of total land use was for the off-farm production of concentrate ingredients (Table 8). In the case of the grass-based system, 11% of total land use was used to produce concentrate (Table 7).

Table 6 shows that total acidification for the grass-based system was on average 42% lower than the confinement system when esti- mated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS. On-farm acidificat- ion for the grass-based system per on-farm area was 71% lower than the confinement system, but for total acidification per total farm area the reduction was 25%. Ammonia accounted for 89% of total acidification in the grass-based system and for 87% in the con- finement system. Table 7 shows that in the grass-based dairy farm, the main contributor to total acidification was NH3 volatilised dur- ing manure application (39.5%), followed by NH3 emitted from cat- tle housing and manure storage (24.5%), and NH3 emitted from excreta deposited by grazing cattle (14%). Manure storage and spreading was the main cause of on-farm acidification. Fertiliser production (57%), electricity generation (16.5%) and fuel combus- tion (13%) were the main sources of off-farm acidification in the grass-based system.

The main contributors to total acidification in the confinement system were NH3 released during cattle housing and manure stor- age (56%) and NH3 released during manure spreading (24%; Table 8). All on-farm acidification was related to storage and use of or- ganic and inorganic fertilisers. Off-farm acidification consisted mainly of NH3 emission from the application of manure to maize silage and concentrate ingredients (36%), emissions from fuel com- bustion (21%) and emissions from electricity (16%) and fertiliser (8.5%) production. In both dairy systems, the contribution of forage production to total acidification was greater than that of concen- trate production (Tables 7 and 8).

Total eutrophication for the grass-based system was on average 72% of the eutrophication for the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area, on-farm eutrophication was 50% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system, but for total eutrophication per total farm area the reduction was 6% (Table 6). In the grass-based system, NO3, NH3, NOx PO and P accounted for 51%, 40%, 3%, 5.5% and 0.5% of total eutrophication, respectively. In the confinement system, NO, NH3, NOx, PO and P accounted for 35%, 49%, 4%, 10% and 2% of total eutrophica- tion, respectively. The main contributor to total eutrophication in

the grass-based system was NO3, PO and P loss during the pro- duction of feed (54%; Table 7). Nitrate, PO and P losses were also the main source of on-farm eutrophication for the grass-based sys- tem (55%) followed by NH3 volatilisation from manure spreading (20%), cattle housing and manure storage (13%), and excreta depos- ited during grazing (7%). Fertiliser production (44%) and NO, PO and P loss from concentrate production (42%) were the main contributors to off-farm eutrophication in the grass-based system. Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the grass-based system (82%; Table 7).

The largest contributor to total eutrophication for the confine- ment system was NO3, PO and P loss during feed production (44%; Table 8), followed by NH3 volatilised during cattle housing and manure storage (32%). Ammonia lost during cattle housing and manure storage (59%) were the main sources of on-farm eutro- phication for the confinement system. Off-farm eutrophication was mainly caused by NO3, PO and P loss during the production of maize silage and concentrate (68%), and NH3 emission from man- ure spreading (12%). Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the confinement system (36%; Table 8).

Fertiliser production (44%) and NO, PO and P loss from concentrate production (42%) were the main contributors to off-farm eutrophication in the grass-based system. Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the grass-based system (82%; Table 7).

The largest contributor to total eutrophication for the confine- ment system was NO3, PO and P loss during feed production (44%; Table 8), followed by NH3 volatilised during cattle housing and manure storage (32%). Ammonia lost during cattle housing and manure storage (59%) were the main sources of on-farm eutro- phication for the confinement system. Off-farm eutrophication was mainly caused by NO3, PO and P loss during the production of maize silage and concentrate (68%), and NH3 emission from man- ure spreading (12%). Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the confinement system (36%; Table 8).
78: Contribution analysis (source percentage of pollutant, resource and environmental impact, e.g. global warming potential) of a seasonal grass-based dairy farm.

Table 8 Contribution analysis (source percentage of pollutant, resource and environmental impact e.g. global warming potential) of a confinement dairy farm.

a NO and NO2. b Non-renewable energy use per MJ. C Global warming potential per kg of CO2 equivalent assuming a 100-year time horizon. d Acidification potential per kg of SO2 equivalent. e Eutrophication potental per kg of PO4 equivalent. f Electricity used for on-farm animal operations, e.g. milking. g Fuel burned during transport, agricultural field operations and processing of concentrate ingredients. h Phosphorous and phosphate loss. i Manure deposited by grazing cattle on pasture. j Emissions and resources used during processing of concentrate ingredients excluding pollutants from fuel combustion and electricity generation.

cows in the confinement system was 8.5% lower (20.6 enteric CH4/kg DMI) relative to cows in the grass-based system (22.5 g en- teric CH4/kg DMI). The main sources of off-farm GHG emission were CO2 from land use change (25%) and fuel combustion (14%), GHG emissions from electricity (22%) and fertiliser (10%) produc- tion, and indirect N20 from NO3 leaching (7%). Total GHG emission from the production of feed in the confinement system was pre- dominately derived from concentrate (32%; Table 8).

Total non-renewable energy use was on average 42% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area and per total farm area, total non-renewable energy use was greatest for the confinement system and least for the grass-based dairy farm. For both systems, the majority of non- renewable energy was consumed during the production of fuel, electricity and fertiliser, ranging from 68% for the confinement sys- tem to 79% for the grass-based system (Tables 7 and 8). In the case of the grass-based system, fertiliser production was the greatest consumer of energy (46%) followed by electricity generation (33%) and fuel combustion (21%). Electricity production was the largest consumer of energy in the confinement system (55.5%) fol- lowed by fuel combustion (31%).

Total land use per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS was on average 23% less for the grass-based system relative to the confine- ment system (Table 6). For the confinement system, 57% of total land use was for the off-farm production of concentrate ingredients (Table 8). In the case of the grass-based system, 11% of total land use was used to produce concentrate (Table 7).

Table 6 shows that total acidification for the grass-based system was on average 42% lower than the confinement system when esti- mated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS. On-farm acidificat- ion for the grass-based system per on-farm area was 71% lower than the confinement system, but for total acidification per total farm area the reduction was 25%. Ammonia accounted for 89% of total acidification in the grass-based system and for 87% in the con- finement system. Table 7 shows that in the grass-based dairy farm, the main contributor to total acidification was NH3 volatilised dur- ing manure application (39.5%), followed by NH3 emitted from cat- tle housing and manure storage (24.5%), and NH3 emitted from excreta deposited by grazing cattle (14%). Manure storage and spreading was the main cause of on-farm acidification. Fertiliser production (57%), electricity generation (16.5%) and fuel combus- tion (13%) were the main sources of off-farm acidification in the grass-based system.

The main contributors to total acidification in the confinement system were NH3 released during cattle housing and manure stor- age (56%) and NH3 released during manure spreading (24%; Table 8). All on-farm acidification was related to storage and use of or- ganic and inorganic fertilisers. Off-farm acidification consisted mainly of NH3 emission from the application of manure to maize silage and concentrate ingredients (36%), emissions from fuel com- bustion (21%) and emissions from electricity (16%) and fertiliser (8.5%) production. In both dairy systems, the contribution of forage production to total acidification was greater than that of concen- trate production (Tables 7 and 8).

Total eutrophication for the grass-based system was on average 72% of the eutrophication for the confinement system when estimated per tonne of FPCM and per tonne of MS (Table 6). Per on-farm area, on-farm eutrophication was 50% lower for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system, but for total eutrophication per total farm area the reduction was 6% (Table 6). In the grass-based system, NO3, NH3, NOx PO and P accounted for 51%, 40%, 3%, 5.5% and 0.5% of total eutrophication, respectively. In the confinement system, NO, NH3, NOx, PO and P accounted for 35%, 49%, 4%, 10% and 2% of total eutrophica- tion, respectively. The main contributor to total eutrophication in

the grass-based system was NO3, PO and P loss during the pro- duction of feed (54%; Table 7). Nitrate, PO and P losses were also the main source of on-farm eutrophication for the grass-based sys- tem (55%) followed by NH3 volatilisation from manure spreading (20%), cattle housing and manure storage (13%), and excreta depos- ited during grazing (7%). Fertiliser production (44%) and NO, PO and P loss from concentrate production (42%) were the main contributors to off-farm eutrophication in the grass-based system. Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the grass-based system (82%; Table 7).

The largest contributor to total eutrophication for the confine- ment system was NO3, PO and P loss during feed production (44%; Table 8), followed by NH3 volatilised during cattle housing and manure storage (32%). Ammonia lost during cattle housing and manure storage (59%) were the main sources of on-farm eutro- phication for the confinement system. Off-farm eutrophication was mainly caused by NO3, PO and P loss during the production of maize silage and concentrate (68%), and NH3 emission from man- ure spreading (12%). Total eutrophication from feed production was mainly from forage in the confinement system (36%; Table 8).

80: The sensitivity of total environmental impacts per kg of FPCM (Table 6) to key assumptions and parameters are shown in Figs. 1 and 2 for the grass-based and confinement systems, respectively. The GHG emission (GWP) of both dairy systems was reduced by including grassland carbon sequestration (S1), but carbon seques- tration had no affect on the remaining environmental impacts. The ranking of GHG emission from dairy systems was not affected by including carbon sequestration. The environmental impacts of both dairy systems were increased (2-3%) when environmental

Fig. 1. The percentage change of the seasonal grass-based dairy system impact assessment results per kg of fat and protein corrected milk for scenario 1 (S1) to scenario 6 (S1 = carbon sequestration included; S2a = economic allocation of impacts between milk and meat; S2b = mass allocation of concentrate ingredients; S3a = Brazilian soybean meal substituted with rape meal S3b; Brazilian soybean meal substituted with US soybean meal; S4 = Enteric CH4 emission tested; S5 = Manure stored in solid system S6 = Nitrous oxide emission from grazing tested). The environmental impacts analysed were global warming potential (GWP), acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), non-renewable energy use (NEU), and land use (L).

Fig. 2. The percentage change of the confinement dairy system impact assessments result per kg of fat and protein corrected milk for scenario 1 (S1) to scenario 6 (S1 = carbon sequestration included; S2a = economic allocation of impacts between milk and meat; S2b = mass allocation of concentrate ingredients; S3a = Brazilian soybean meal substituted with rape meal S3b; Brazilian soybean meal substituted with US soybean meal; S4 = Enteric CH4 emission tested; S5 = Manure stored in solid system S6 = Nitrous oxide emission from grazing tested). The environmental impacts analysed were global warming potential (GWP), acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), non-renewable energy use (NEU), and land use (L).

impacts were allocated between milk and meat based on their economic value (S2a). However, the increase was similar for both systems. Thus, the comparison of environmental impacts between dairy systems was not sensitive to economic allocation rules for milk and meat. The environmental impacts of both dairy systems were also increased when impacts were allocated between concen- trate co-products based on the mass of co-products (S2b). The in- creases in environmental impacts in S2b were greater for the confinement system (Fig. 2) compared to the grass-based system (Fig. 1), because of the greater quantity of concentrate fed. Thus, mass allocation of concentrate co-products did not affect the rank- ing of environmental impacts between dairy systems.

Substituting Brazilian soybean meal with rape meal (S3a) or soybean meal from the USA (S3b) caused a slight decline (<2%) or did not affect the environmental impacts of the grass-based sys- tem (Fig. 1). Replacing Brazilian soybean meal with rape meal (S3a) or soybean meal from the USA (S3b) in the confinement system re- duced all impacts except energy use (Fig. 2). Rape meal (S3a) par- ticularly reduced land use (18%) and the GWP (9.5%) of the confinement system. Soybean meal from the USA particularly re- duced (9%) the GWP of the confinement system. However, replac- ing soybean meal with alternative protein meals did not affect the ranking of environmental impacts between dairy systems.

The GWP of the grass-based system was slightly reduced (1%) when measured instead of predicted enteric CH4 emission (S4) was used to estimate CH4 emission from dairy cows (Fig. 1). The predicted enteric CH4 emission overestimated the measured en- teric CH4 measured from dairy cows in April and May by 21.5%. In contrast, in the confinement system the predicted enteric CH4 emission overestimated the measured enteric CH4 measured by <1%. Thus, the GWP of the confinement system was not affected using measured enteric CH4 emission in S4 (Fig. 2). Measured en- teric CH4 emission did not affect the comparison of environmental impacts between systems.

Storing manure in solid rather than liquid systems (S5) caused a greater reduction in environmental impacts for the confinement system relative to the grass-based system, because of the longer housing period (Figs. 1 and 2). However, even though the reduction was greater for the confinement system compared to the grass- based system, the comparison of environmental impacts between dairy systems was not affected. Increasing the N20 emission factor for manure deposited during grazing (S6) increased the GWP of both systems, particularly the grass-based system (Fig. 1). The comparison of GHG emission between dairy systems was not sen- sitive to the increase in the N20 emission factor for manure ex- creted during grazing.
