87: The purpose of this study was to develop an LCA model to assess the environmental impact of a confinement dairy system and a seasonal grass-based dairy system. The model was successfully developed and the results indicated that the higher milk produc- tion in the confinement system required more resources and re- leased more pollutants for a given level of milk compared to the intensively grazed seasonal grass-based system. However, the out- comes for both systems were affected by assumptions and deci- sions e.g. carbon cycle assumptions and allocation rules made during the LCA modelling process.

Comparison of results between LCA studies is difficult due to dif- ferences in computation of life cycle inventories and choice of func- tional unit (de Boer, 2003; Van der Werf et al., 2009). Therefore, direct numerical comparisons were not made. Instead, within case study comparisons of grass-based and confinement dairy systems were made to analyse differences in environmental impacts. Envi- ronmental impacts were quantified per unit area as well as per unit of milk firstly, because impact categories, acidification and eutrophi- cation, have a local and regional effect (Thomassen and de Boer, 2005) and secondly, because national inventories of GHG emissions are quantified in isolation from food production, e.g. total GHG emis- sion/nation state (Casey and Holden, 2005; Crosson et al., 2011).

Similar to previous LCA studies of grass and confinement dairy systems, CH4 from enteric fermentation was identified as the main cause of on-farm GHG emission (Arsenault et al., 2009; Casey and Holden, 2005). Feed consumption is the main driver of enteric CH4 emission (Hegarty et al., 2007) and explained the greater on-farm GHG emission per on-farm area from the confinement system (618 t DMI/herd per annum) compared to the grass-based system (526 t DMI/herd per annum). However, per unit of milk, on-farm GHG emission was greater for the grass-based system. This was be- cause the animal diet in the confinement system contained less forage. Therefore, enteric CH4 emission per kg of DMI was greater for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system.

Greenhouse gas emissions from the cultivation of purchased concentrate and forage, and emissions associated with the manu- facture of inputs, e.g. fertiliser used in the production of on-farm forages, were the main contributors to off-farm GHG emission. Off-farm GHG emission from the confinement system were more than double the off-farm GHG emission from the grass-based sys- tem, because of the greater quantity of concentrate fed. Conse- quently, total GHG emission per unit of milk was greater for the confinement system relative to the grass-based system in agree- ment with previous findings (Rotz et al., 2009; Van der Nagel et al., 2003). Interestingly, total GHG emission per total farm area (on-farm and off-farm land) was lower for the confinement system relative to the grass-based system. This unexpected result occurred firstly, because of the large area required for the off-farm produc-
tion of key concentrate ingredients fed, soybean meal DM) and barley DM), and secondly, because of the high productivity of temperate grasslands (Jones and Donnelly, 2004).

Total land use per unit of milk was greater for the confinement farm compared to the grass-based farm. This outcome disagrees with the findings of Arsenault et al. (2009), but is supported by the results of Basset-Mens et al. (2009b) and Van der Nagel et al. (2003). The disagreement between studies was caused by differ- ences in pasture yields, ranging from 9 to 14 tonnes DM/ha, differ- ences in concentrate composition and quantities fed, and differences in the allocation of impacts between concentrate co- products. Congruous with previous studies, the majority of total non-renewable energy use occurred off-farm during the produc- tion of fertilisers, fuel and electricity, and the transport of pur- chased inputs to the farm (Thomassen et al., 2008b; Van der Werf et al., 2009). As a consequence of the greater dependence of the confinement system on imported inputs compared to the grass-based system, the confinement system used more total non-renewable energy per unit of milk and per unit area. However, this also resulted in the confinement system using less non-renew- able energy on-farm per unit of milk, because less feed was pro- duced on-farm compared to the grass-based system.

Ammonia volatilised on-farm from the management of manure was the main cause of on-farm and total acidification for both sys- tems and this agrees with previous dairy LCA studies (Arsenault et al., 2009; Van der Werf et al., 2009). The lower on-farm and total acidification per unit of milk and per unit area for the grass-based system compared to the confinement system implies that, reduc- ing or eliminating manure storage and the cattle housing period, results in lower acidification per unit of milk and per unit area. The reduction or removal of manure storage also reduced CH4 a significant contributor to GHG emission in confinement systems (Arsenault et al., 2009; Rotz et al., 2010).

Nitrate and P loss following the application of non-organic and organic fertiliser to forage, and NO3 leaching from excreta depos- ited by cattle on pasture were found in this, and previous LCA stud- ies (Basset-Mens et al., 2009b; Thomassen et al., 2008b), to be the main contributors to on-farm eutrophication for grass-based dairy systems. For confinement systems, this study and Arsenault et al. (2009), found that NH3 emission from manure management and NO3 and P loss during forage production were the main contribu- tors to on-farm eutrophication. In contrast to the acidification re- sults, the shorter manure storage period of the grass-based system did not result in lower on-farm eutrophication per unit of milk relative to the confinement system. This was because a major contributor to on-farm eutrophication, feed production, was lower in the confinement system compared to the grass-based system. On-farm eutrophication from feed production was lower in the confinement system, because more feed was imported in the con- finement system.

Including off-farm eutrophic emissions from purchased feed re- sulted in greater total eutrophication per unit of milk for the con- finement system relative to the grass-based system. This implies that feeding less concentrate reduces N and P loss from feed pro- duction, in agreement with Thomassen et al. (2008b). On-farm eutrophication per on-farm area and total eutrophication per unit area were greater for the confinement system compared to the grass-based system. This was because the confinement system produced more milk per on-farm area than the grass-based system and total eutrophic emission per unit of milk was greater for the confinement system compared to the grass-based system.

This was because a major contributor to on-farm eutrophication, feed production, was lower in the confinement system compared to the grass-based system. On-farm eutrophication from feed production was lower in the confinement system, because more feed was imported in the con- finement system.

Including off-farm eutrophic emissions from purchased feed re- sulted in greater total eutrophication per unit of milk for the con- finement system relative to the grass-based system. This implies that feeding less concentrate reduces N and P loss from feed pro- duction, in agreement with Thomassen et al. (2008b). On-farm eutrophication per on-farm area and total eutrophication per unit area were greater for the confinement system compared to the grass-based system. This was because the confinement system produced more milk per on-farm area than the grass-based system and total eutrophic emission per unit of milk was greater for the confinement system compared to the grass-based system.
90: The purpose of this study was to develop an LCA model to assess the environmental impact of a confinement dairy system and a seasonal grass-based dairy system. The model was successfully developed and the results indicated that the higher milk produc- tion in the confinement system required more resources and re- leased more pollutants for a given level of milk compared to the intensively grazed seasonal grass-based system. However, the out- comes for both systems were affected by assumptions and deci- sions e.g. carbon cycle assumptions and allocation rules made during the LCA modelling process.

Comparison of results between LCA studies is difficult due to dif- ferences in computation of life cycle inventories and choice of func- tional unit (de Boer, 2003; Van der Werf et al., 2009). Therefore, direct numerical comparisons were not made. Instead, within case study comparisons of grass-based and confinement dairy systems were made to analyse differences in environmental impacts. Envi- ronmental impacts were quantified per unit area as well as per unit of milk firstly, because impact categories, acidification and eutrophi- cation, have a local and regional effect (Thomassen and de Boer, 2005) and secondly, because national inventories of GHG emissions are quantified in isolation from food production, e.g. total GHG emis- sion/nation state (Casey and Holden, 2005; Crosson et al., 2011).

Similar to previous LCA studies of grass and confinement dairy systems, CH4 from enteric fermentation was identified as the main cause of on-farm GHG emission (Arsenault et al., 2009; Casey and Holden, 2005). Feed consumption is the main driver of enteric CH4 emission (Hegarty et al., 2007) and explained the greater on-farm GHG emission per on-farm area from the confinement system (618 t DMI/herd per annum) compared to the grass-based system (526 t DMI/herd per annum). However, per unit of milk, on-farm GHG emission was greater for the grass-based system. This was be- cause the animal diet in the confinement system contained less forage. Therefore, enteric CH4 emission per kg of DMI was greater for the grass-based system relative to the confinement system.

Greenhouse gas emissions from the cultivation of purchased concentrate and forage, and emissions associated with the manu- facture of inputs, e.g. fertiliser used in the production of on-farm forages, were the main contributors to off-farm GHG emission. Off-farm GHG emission from the confinement system were more than double the off-farm GHG emission from the grass-based sys- tem, because of the greater quantity of concentrate fed. Conse- quently, total GHG emission per unit of milk was greater for the confinement system relative to the grass-based system in agree- ment with previous findings (Rotz et al., 2009; Van der Nagel et al., 2003). Interestingly, total GHG emission per total farm area (on-farm and off-farm land) was lower for the confinement system relative to the grass-based system. This unexpected result occurred firstly, because of the large area required for the off-farm produc-
tion of key concentrate ingredients fed, soybean meal DM) and barley DM), and secondly, because of the high productivity of temperate grasslands (Jones and Donnelly, 2004).

Total land use per unit of milk was greater for the confinement farm compared to the grass-based farm. This outcome disagrees with the findings of Arsenault et al. (2009), but is supported by the results of Basset-Mens et al. (2009b) and Van der Nagel et al. (2003). The disagreement between studies was caused by differ- ences in pasture yields, ranging from 9 to 14 tonnes DM/ha, differ- ences in concentrate composition and quantities fed, and differences in the allocation of impacts between concentrate co- products. Congruous with previous studies, the majority of total non-renewable energy use occurred off-farm during the produc- tion of fertilisers, fuel and electricity, and the transport of pur- chased inputs to the farm (Thomassen et al., 2008b; Van der Werf et al., 2009). As a consequence of the greater dependence of the confinement system on imported inputs compared to the grass-based system, the confinement system used more total non-renewable energy per unit of milk and per unit area. However, this also resulted in the confinement system using less non-renew- able energy on-farm per unit of milk, because less feed was pro- duced on-farm compared to the grass-based system.

Ammonia volatilised on-farm from the management of manure was the main cause of on-farm and total acidification for both sys- tems and this agrees with previous dairy LCA studies (Arsenault et al., 2009; Van der Werf et al., 2009). The lower on-farm and total acidification per unit of milk and per unit area for the grass-based system compared to the confinement system implies that, reduc- ing or eliminating manure storage and the cattle housing period, results in lower acidification per unit of milk and per unit area. The reduction or removal of manure storage also reduced CH4 a significant contributor to GHG emission in confinement systems (Arsenault et al., 2009; Rotz et al., 2010).

Nitrate and P loss following the application of non-organic and organic fertiliser to forage, and NO3 leaching from excreta depos- ited by cattle on pasture were found in this, and previous LCA stud- ies (Basset-Mens et al., 2009b; Thomassen et al., 2008b), to be the main contributors to on-farm eutrophication for grass-based dairy systems. Nitrate and P loss following the application of non-organic and organic fertiliser to forage, and NO3 leaching from excreta depos- ited by cattle on pasture were found in this, and previous LCA stud- ies (Basset-Mens et al., 2009b; Thomassen et al., 2008b), to be the main contributors to on-farm eutrophication for grass-based dairy systems. For confinement systems, this study and Arsenault et al. (2009), found that NH3 emission from manure management and NO3 and P loss during forage production were the main contribu- tors to on-farm eutrophication. In contrast to the acidification re- sults, the shorter manure storage period of the grass-based system did not result in lower on-farm eutrophication per unit of milk relative to the confinement system. This was because a major contributor to on-farm eutrophication, feed production, was lower in the confinement system compared to the grass-based system. On-farm eutrophication from feed production was lower in the confinement system, because more feed was imported in the con- finement system.

Including off-farm eutrophic emissions from purchased feed re- sulted in greater total eutrophication per unit of milk for the con- finement system relative to the grass-based system. This implies that feeding less concentrate reduces N and P loss from feed pro- duction, in agreement with Thomassen et al. (2008b). On-farm eutrophication per on-farm area and total eutrophication per unit area were greater for the confinement system compared to the grass-based system. This was because the confinement system produced more milk per on-farm area than the grass-based system and total eutrophic emission per unit of milk was greater for the confinement system compared to the grass-based system.
95: The higher environmental impact of concentrate compared to forage is in broad agreement with previous dairy LCA studies

(Arsenault et al., 2009; Thomassen et al., 2008b). Therefore, the confinement system could be improved by reducing concentrate feed use or using concentrate ingredients with a lower environ- mental impact. For example, the results of this and previous work (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000) suggest that the environmental impact of concentrate feed could be reduced by using domestic or regionally produced rapeseed meal instead of imported soybean meal. The impact of concentrate could also be reduced through careful diet formulation e.g. avoiding the use of high protein con- centrate feed. This would reduce nitrogen losses, which are a major cause of many environmental impacts (global warming, acidificat- ion and eutrophication).

Manure is a major source of CH4 and NH3 emissions (Hyde et al., 2003; Rotz et al., 2010). Thus, another strategy to reduce the envi- ronmental impact of dairy systems is to eliminate or improve man- ure storage. Potential strategies to reduce emissions from this source for grass-based systems include increasing the length of the grazing season (Lovett et al., 2008), or as this and previous work (Amon et al., 2001) found, storing manure under aerobic con- ditions, e.g. solid manure systems. In confinement systems, adopt- ing aerobic storage systems, covering manure stores and flaring biogas, and increasing the frequency of manure removal are poten- tial approaches to reduce emissions from stored manure (Rotz et al., 2010; Weiske et al., 2006). Inorganic N fertilisers are a major cause of environmental impacts for forage and concentrate produc- tion. Dairy farmers can reduce the environmental impact and re- source use of milk production by carefully targeting and reducing N fertiliser application to forage produced on-farm. This strategy can also be applied to concentrate ingredients, but generally can- not be implemented by dairy farmers. Producers can also incorpo- rate nitrogen-fixing legumes into pasture. This reduces inorganic N fertiliser requirements (Luo et al., 2010), thereby reducing the im- pact of grass production.

97: The reliability of outcomes of "cradle to farm-gate" dairy farm LCA studies are affected by the quality of data available, emission factors, modelling assumptions and choices, and sample size. Infor- mation gathered for LCA studies are not always accurate. For example, inaccuracies occur because data were originally collected for other purposes, e.g. financial accounts (Thomassen et al., 2008b). However, because LCA studies require large amounts of data, the practitioner may have no other option but to use such data sources. Emission factors from published research and inter- national databases are used in LCA studies to estimate emissions, because measuring all emissions is too difficult and expensive. Therefore, LCA outcomes are a measure of the potential environ- mental impact and not the actual environmental impact. Actual environmental impacts differ from the potential due to variability of numerous local factors, for example, soil type, climate and topography (de Boer, 2003).

Many assumptions and choices are made during the LCA mod- elling process. Assumptions and decisions that were present in this and previous studies that have an important influence on the re- sults, were carbon sequestration and allocation rules for concen- trate ingredients (Rotz et al. 2009, 2010; Thomassen et al. 2008a). These findings regarding carbon sequestration highlight the requirement for further research on soil carbon and for future dairy LCA studies to clearly outline carbon cycle assumptions through a complete carbon balance. We measured a large effect of using different allocation rules and this agrees with present ISO recommendations that allocation should be avoided by expanding the system boundaries to include the production of the avoided product (system expansion). Previous studies of dairy systems have generally used system expansion to avoid allocation

between milk and meat. These studies expand the system bound- aries of dairy systems using the "avoided burdens" approach where the environmental impacts of the alternative way of pro- ducing the co-product meat are subtracted from dairy production (Cederberg and Stadig, 2003; Thomassen et al., 2008a). The results of these studies have shown that beef production from pure beef systems has a greater environmental impact than beef from dairy systems. Therefore, assuming constant demand for milk and beef, increasing milk yield per cow increases environmental impacts from dairy and beef production, because more beef cows are re- quired to compensate fewer dairy cows (Cederberg and Stadig, 2003; Zehetmeier et al., in press). If allocation was avoided in this study these findings suggest that the grass-based system would have caused a greater reduction in environmental impacts relative to the confinement system, because cows in the confinement sys- tem produced less surplus calves and meat per unit of milk.

Additional important decisions made during the modelling pro- cess are the database or literature source used to source emission factors for off-farm inputs and characterisation factors used to cal- culate environmental impacts. Emission factors for equivalent off- farm inputs e.g. fertiliser from databases and literature sources can be very different. For example, the Ecoinvent database estimates, that a kilogram of Brazilian soybean meal emits 1.4 kg of CO2 equivalents, but a recent FAO report, (using the same GWP factors and time horizon) estimated that 7.7 kg of CO2 equivalent are emit- ted per kilogram of Brazilian soybean meal (Gerber et al., 2010). This variation occurred because of different assumptions regarding the role of soybean in deforestation. Similarly, characterisation fac- tors used during the impact assessment stage can vary signifi- cantly, because of different decisions regarding time horizons (Basset-Mens et al., 2009a). These important modelling assump- tions and decisions highlight the need for further standardisation of LCA procedures to obtain more robust outcomes.

Comparing different systems of production that produce equiv- alent products requires a large sample size and large amounts of data with a high degree of accuracy (Basset-Mens and Van der Werf, 2005). Within this study, only two individual production sys- tems were investigated, because high quality data were unavail- able for a large number of farms. Therefore, the farming systems investigated are not representative of total Irish seasonal grass- based and confinement dairy systems. Although this is a shortcom- ing of this study, it is not uncommon in dairy LCA studies. Because most dairy LCA studies are not representative of the total popula- tion due to inadequate sample size or that dairy farms were not chosen at random (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Thomassen et al., 2008b; Van der Werf et al., 2009).

102: This LCA study indicates that a seasonal grass-based system can have a lower environmental impact (potential resource use and pollutants), for a given level of milk and per farmland area, com- pared to a confinement system. The greater environmental impact of the confinement system was explained by the higher resource use and pollutants from the production of concentrate feed com- pared to forage, and the longer manure storage period. Measures identified to reduce the environmental impact of both systems in- clude decreasing the manure storage period and concentrate feed use, reducing the use of concentrates with a high environmental impact, and using aerobic manure storage. The findings of our anal- ysis also indicate that the results of dairy LCA studies are affected by carbon sink assumptions and allocation rules. Thus, more re- search on soil carbon sequestration and further international stan- dardisation of the LCA procedure are required. This LCA study is one of the few to directly compare the environmental impact of

a grass-based and a confinement dairy system. However, direct comparisons, with a greater number of farms representative of grass-based and confinement systems, are required to obtain a more robust comparison of these two production systems at a re- gional or national scale.
